questions.scenario: Harriet purchases a Victorian townhouse from Gerald. During the sale, the contract is silent as to a large antique mirror mounted into an ornate plaster alcove that was specifically designed to frame it, and a freestanding mahogany wardrobe with ornate feet, which rests on the floor without any fixings. Gerald removes both items before completion. Harriet's solicitor also discovers that a plasma television, bolted to the wall via a bracket and connected to a bespoke hidden cable system routed through the walls, was also removed by Gerald.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the legal position regarding all three items?

options.A: The mirror and television are fixtures and must be returned; the wardrobe is a chattel. The mirror was incorporated into the architectural design; the television was annexed via a bespoke cable system to enhance the building; the wardrobe is freestanding.
options.B: All three items are chattels because Gerald retained them before completion, which demonstrates his intention that they should not pass with the land.
options.C: The mirror and wardrobe are chattels because their purpose is the better enjoyment of the items as chattels rather than to enhance the building; the television is a fixture and must be returned.
options.D: All three items are fixtures because they were all physically present in the building at the time of contract, and s 62 LPA 1925 prevents Gerald from removing anything from the property.
options.E: The mirror and television are fixtures and must be returned to Harriet; the wardrobe is a chattel and Gerald may keep it.

questions.explanation: A is correct. The two-part test from Holland v Hodgson applies: degree of annexation and, taking priority, purpose of annexation (Berkley v Poulett). The mirror was incorporated into the architectural design of the alcove (D'Eyncourt v Gregory), making it a fixture regardless of how easily it could be removed. The television was bolted into the wall with a bespoke cable system routed through the structure, showing purpose was to enhance the building rather than enjoy the chattel as such (Leigh v Taylor distinguishes this), so it is a fixture. The wardrobe is freestanding and its purpose is the convenient use of the chattel itself — it is a chattel. Under s 62 LPA 1925, a conveyance automatically passes all fixtures unless specifically excluded under s 62(4), so Gerald must return both fixtures. B: Incorrect — the seller's act of removal does not determine chattel or fixture status; the legal test is objective. E: Incorrect as to the television — it correctly identifies the mirror but mischaracterises the television as excluded. D: Incorrect — s 62 passes fixtures, not all items physically present, and the wardrobe is a chattel. C: Incorrect — the mirror was built into the architectural design and its purpose was to enhance the building, not to enjoy the mirror as a chattel.

questions.scenario: Tomasz owns a freehold property. He allows his friend Renata to park her car in his garage under a verbal agreement for which she pays a modest monthly sum. He also grants his neighbour Sylvia a formal right of way over his rear garden, created by deed and registered at the Land Registry. Tomasz later sells the freehold to a developer, Hallmark Estates Ltd, without telling either Renata or Sylvia. Hallmark immediately padlocks the garage and blocks the path across the rear garden.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Renata and Sylvia on their respective legal positions against Hallmark Estates Ltd?

options.A: Renata has no proprietary right enforceable against Hallmark because her arrangement is a personal licence only; Sylvia's registered easement is a proprietary right that binds any purchaser under s 29(2) LRA 2002.
options.B: Neither Renata nor Sylvia can enforce their respective rights against Hallmark because Tomasz did not inform Hallmark of either arrangement before the sale.
options.C: Both Renata and Sylvia have proprietary rights that are enforceable against Hallmark; Renata's parking arrangement was an easement by prescription and Sylvia's right of way is a registered easement.
options.D: Renata can enforce her parking arrangement against Hallmark because she paid money for it, giving it contractual and therefore proprietary effect; Sylvia's right of way also binds Hallmark.
options.E: Sylvia's right of way is enforceable against Hallmark, but only if she can prove she has exercised it within the last year; Renata can also enforce her licence if she has paid at least two years of monthly sums.

questions.explanation: A is correct. A licence is a personal right only — it cannot be enforced against a third party such as Hallmark (as illustrated by the 'house by the sea' example in W1 notes). Renata's verbal parking arrangement is a licence, not an easement: there is no deed, no registration, and a verbal agreement for parking with no formal grant cannot satisfy the formality requirements for a legal easement. Sylvia's right of way was created by deed and registered under s 27(2)(d) LRA 2002, making it a legal easement that appears on Hallmark's title and binds any purchaser under s 29(2) LRA 2002. B: Incorrect — prescription requires 20 years' use; there is no evidence of this, and a verbal informal arrangement is not a prescriptive claim. E: Incorrect — payment of money does not convert a licence into a proprietary right; the distinction between proprietary and personal rights turns on nature, creation and protection, not payment alone. C: Incorrect — Tomasz's disclosure to Hallmark is irrelevant to the position of Sylvia's registered easement, which binds by virtue of registration. D: Incorrect — a properly registered express legal easement binds automatically; there is no annual-use condition for enforcement against Hallmark.

questions.scenario: Orlanda owns a registered freehold property in the city. A neighbouring developer constructs a tower block and the construction crane repeatedly swings its jib over Orlanda's property during working hours at a height of approximately 15 metres above the roof of her house. Orlanda also discovers that a water company, acting under statutory authority, has laid a new pipe at a depth of 350 metres beneath her garden without her knowledge or consent. Orlanda wishes to take action in respect of both intrusions.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes Orlanda's legal position in respect of both the crane and the underground pipe?

options.A: Orlanda cannot bring a trespass claim for the crane because aircraft have immunity from trespass claims; she may have a claim in nuisance for the underground pipe.
options.B: Orlanda can seek an injunction for the crane only if she can demonstrate that the jib has caused her quantifiable damage; the underground pipe is not a trespass if the water company has statutory authority.
options.C: Orlanda can bring a trespass claim in respect of both the crane jib and the underground pipe because the maxim 'cuius est solum' means she owns everything above and below the surface, with no statutory limits.
options.D: Orlanda can bring a trespass claim for the crane jib because it intrudes into her lower airspace; she has no claim for the underground pipe because s 43 Infrastructure Act 2015 removes trespass liability below 300 metres.
options.E: Orlanda has no claim in respect of either intrusion because the upper airspace and deep subsoil are excluded from the definition of land.

questions.explanation: D is correct. A landowner's rights in the lower airspace — that portion necessary for the ordinary use and enjoyment of the land and structures on it — are protected as part of the land. A crane jib swinging at 15 metres above the roof intrudes into the lower airspace and constitutes a trespass regardless of whether damage is caused (Kelsen v Imperial Tobacco; Anchor Brewhouse v Berkley House). In contrast, s 43 Infrastructure Act 2015 provides that there is no trespass in respect of activity at or below 300 metres, so Orlanda has no claim for the 350-metre pipe. E: Incorrect — the lower airspace is part of Orlanda's land and she does have rights over it. C: Incorrect — the maxim is qualified by statute; s 43 Infrastructure Act 2015 removes her rights below 300 metres. A: Incorrect — the s 76(1) Civil Aviation Act immunity applies to aircraft in flight, not construction cranes. B: Incorrect — trespass to airspace in the lower zone does not require proof of damage (Kelsen); an injunction can be sought regardless, as demonstrated where a sign projecting only a few inches was injuncted.

questions.scenario: Benedikt orally agrees to sell his registered freehold property to Claudette for £450,000. They shake hands and Claudette pays a £45,000 deposit. Six weeks later, before any written contract is signed and before any transfer deed is executed, Benedikt changes his mind and refuses to proceed. Claudette also discovers that, two years ago, Benedikt granted his business partner a right of drainage over the property by a signed written document described as 'an agreement for an easement', but that document was never submitted to the Land Registry.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the legal position on both the sale and the drainage right?

options.A: Claudette has a binding contract because she paid a deposit; she can seek specific performance in equity. The drainage right is a legal easement because it was made in writing.
options.B: Claudette has no binding contract because s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 requires a land contract to be in writing, contain all agreed terms, and be signed by both parties; the drainage right may be equitable if it satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989.
options.C: The oral agreement creates an implied trust in Claudette's favour which is enforceable against Benedikt. The drainage right is a valid equitable easement because the written agreement satisfies s 53(1)(a) LPA 1925.
options.D: Claudette has no binding contract; she can only recover the deposit in damages. The drainage right is void for all purposes because it was not registered.
options.E: Claudette has a binding contract to purchase the property because the oral agreement and payment of a deposit together satisfy the requirements of s 2 LP(MP)A 1989; the drainage right is a valid legal easement.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under s 2 LP(MP)A 1989, a contract for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land must be in writing, incorporate all agreed terms, and be signed by both parties. An oral agreement with a handshake and deposit does not satisfy these requirements, so there is no binding contract. On the drainage right: to be legal, an easement over registered land must be created by deed and registered under s 27(2)(d) LRA 2002 (and comply with s 52 LPA 1925 and s 1 LP(MP)A 1989 for the deed). A written agreement described as 'an agreement' is not a deed. However, an easement that fails to become legal may still be equitable if it complies with s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 (in writing, all terms, signed by both parties) — equity treats it as a contract to grant a legal easement. D: Incorrect — s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 requires writing, not merely oral agreement; a deposit alone does not cure this. A: Incorrect — writing is a necessary formality; payment of a deposit is insufficient. The drainage right is not legal without a deed and registration. C: Incorrect — the drainage right is not necessarily void for all purposes; it may be equitable if the document satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989. E: Incorrect — equity does not impose a trust from a bare oral agreement for sale; s 53(1)(a) LPA 1925 governs the creation of express trusts, not equitable easements; the correct provision for a failed legal easement operating in equity is s 2 LP(MP)A 1989.

questions.tag: Equity of Redemption and Undue Influence
questions.scenario: Portia is a sole trader who owns a registered freehold. She approaches Greenfields Bank for a business loan of £300,000. The bank's standard mortgage terms include a clause postponing the legal date of redemption for 25 years and a collateral advantage requiring that Portia purchase all her office supplies exclusively from a subsidiary of the bank for the duration of the mortgage at prices 30% above market rate. Portia's solicitor also advises her that her husband Marcus, who has no ownership interest in the property, was pressured by Portia to consent to the mortgage by threatening to end their marriage.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Portia and the bank on the validity of the mortgage terms and on Marcus's position?

options.A: The postponement clause may be valid if it does not make the mortgage irredeemable in substance; the collateral advantage is likely void as unconscionable; Marcus's consent is irrelevant as he has no ownership interest.
options.B: The bank can enforce the entire mortgage because Portia is a commercially sophisticated borrower and equity does not intervene in commercial transactions; Marcus has a right to occupy the property under Class F.
options.C: Both the postponement clause and the collateral advantage are automatically void as clogs on the equity of redemption; Marcus's consent cannot be set aside because he chose to enter it freely.
options.D: The postponement clause and the collateral advantage are both valid because commercial borrowers are treated differently from residential borrowers in equity; Marcus has no locus standi as he has no ownership interest.
options.E: The postponement clause is void because it exceeds ten years; the collateral advantage is valid as a legitimate commercial arrangement; Marcus can set aside the mortgage as a non-owning spouse under the Family Law Act 1996.

questions.explanation: A is correct. On postponement: equity will not allow a clause that makes redemption illusory or a fetter on the equity of redemption (Fairclough v Swan Brewery; Knightsbridge Estates Trust v Byrne). A 25-year postponement on a commercial mortgage is not automatically void — courts have more latitude with commercial transactions — but it must not render the mortgage irredeemable in substance. On collateral advantages: the key question is whether the advantage is unconscionable or commercially unreasonable. A requirement to purchase supplies at 30% above market rate for the full duration is likely an unconscionable collateral advantage which equity will strike out. On Marcus: Marcus has no ownership interest in the property and therefore has no equity-of-redemption rights. His consent would only be relevant if he were a co-owner or had an overriding equitable interest capable of binding a purchaser — he does not. D: Incorrect — commercial borrowers are not entirely exempt from equitable protection; unconscionable collateral advantages can still be struck down. C: Incorrect — not all postponement clauses are automatically void; courts apply a 'question of fact and degree' test. E: Incorrect — there is no fixed 10-year rule for postponement. B: Incorrect — equity does intervene even in commercial mortgage transactions where terms are unconscionable; a Class F charge protects occupation rights for non-owning spouses but is not relevant here.

questions.tag: Power of Sale and Lender's Duties
questions.scenario: Halcyon Finance Ltd holds a registered legal mortgage over a commercial property owned by Dermot. The mortgage was created by deed and registered. Dermot falls into arrears: he misses one monthly capital repayment of £1,200. Halcyon serves no notice but immediately instructs estate agents to sell the property by private treaty. The agents recommend marketing for 12 weeks, but Halcyon instructs them to sell within two weeks. The property sells for £820,000. An independent valuation obtained after the sale shows the market value at the time of sale was between £970,000 and £1,050,000.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Dermot on his rights against Halcyon?

options.A: Halcyon exercised the power of sale lawfully because the power arose and was exercisable once Dermot missed one payment; however, it is liable in damages for breach of its duty to obtain the true market value.
options.B: Halcyon's power of sale has arisen under s 101(1)(i) LPA 1925 because mortgage money has become due, but it was not yet exercisable under s 103 because none of the three triggering criteria had been satisfied.
options.C: Halcyon cannot exercise any power of sale without a court order because Dermot only missed one payment.
options.D: The sale to the purchaser is voidable at Dermot's option because the power of sale was exercised prematurely; Halcyon must return the property to Dermot.
options.E: Halcyon has validly exercised the power of sale because the power arose when Dermot missed a capital repayment, and lenders have unfettered discretion as to the timing and method of sale.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The statutory power of sale under s 101(1)(i) LPA 1925 arises when 'mortgage money has become due' — this occurs when one capital repayment is missed (Payne v Cardiff), so the power has arisen. However, the power is not exercisable until at least one of the s 103 criteria is satisfied: (1) notice requiring repayment of the whole loan has been served and the borrower has defaulted; (2) interest is in arrears for at least two months; or (3) there has been a breach of another mortgage provision. Missing one capital repayment does not satisfy any of these criteria — particularly criterion (2) which requires interest arrears, not capital. No notice was served. Therefore the power was not exercisable. Under s 104 LPA 1925, a sale after the power has arisen but before it is exercisable is valid against an innocent purchaser but leaves the lender liable in damages to the borrower. On the duty of care: Cuckmere Brick establishes that lenders owe a duty to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value. Selling in two weeks against agent advice, achieving £820,000 against a value of £970,000–£1,050,000, suggests a breach. E: Incorrect — lenders have unfettered discretion on timing (Silven), but this does not override the requirement that the power be exercisable, nor does it eliminate the Cuckmere duty. C: Incorrect — a court order is not required for exercise of the power of sale; it is a right of the lender. A: Incorrect — the power was not exercisable because no s 103 criteria were met. D: Incorrect — under s 104, a sale to an innocent purchaser is valid; Dermot cannot recover the property but can claim damages.

questions.scenario: Verity and her partner Jed are co-owners of a registered freehold property, holding the legal title as joint tenants. Without Jed's knowledge, Verity approaches Apex Lending and purports to mortgage her 'share' of the property to secure a personal loan of £80,000. The document Verity signs is described as a 'charge over my beneficial interest', is in writing and signed only by Verity. Apex does not register any interest at the Land Registry. Six months later, Verity and Jed sell the freehold to a purchaser, Gemma, who pays the purchase price to both Verity and Jed as joint legal owners.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the legal position of Apex Lending following the sale to Gemma?

options.A: Apex holds an equitable mortgage over Verity's beneficial interest created under s 53(1)(c) LPA 1925
options.B: Apex holds a legal mortgage binding Gemma because Verity, as a joint legal owner, had authority to mortgage the entire freehold without Jed's consent.
options.C: Apex has no interest at all because Verity cannot mortgage property she does not legally own, and the document is invalid for want of a deed.
options.D: Apex holds a valid legal mortgage over the property that binds Gemma because it was created in writing and signed by Verity.
options.E: Apex has a valid equitable mortgage which binds Gemma because Gemma had constructive notice of the mortgage as a result of Verity's occupation of the property.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Verity cannot grant a legal mortgage over her 'share' because the legal title is held jointly and cannot be dealt with by one joint tenant alone; additionally, there is no deed and no registration, so this cannot be a legal mortgage. However, under s 53(1)(c) LPA 1925, a mortgage of an equitable interest requires writing signed by the grantor — this is satisfied. So Apex holds an equitable mortgage over Verity's beneficial interest. On Gemma: co-owned land is held on trust under TOLATA. A beneficiary's equitable interest can be overreached if the purchase price is paid to at least two trustees (ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925). Here, Gemma paid to both Verity and Jed (the two legal owners/trustees), so overreaching operates. Apex's interest shifts from the land to Verity's share of the sale proceeds — Gemma takes free. C: Incorrect — there is no deed and no registration; the document cannot create a legal mortgage. D: Incorrect — an equitable mortgage of a beneficial interest can be created under s 53(1)(c) without a deed. E: Incorrect — overreaching defeats the equitable interest regardless of notice; Gemma is not bound. B: Incorrect — a joint legal owner cannot unilaterally mortgage the full legal estate without the other owner's consent; this is a mortgage of the beneficial interest only.

questions.scenario: Fenwick Bank holds a registered legal mortgage over Oona's residential freehold property. Oona has fallen significantly into arrears over 18 months. Fenwick seeks an order for possession. Oona applies to the court under the Administration of Justice Act 1970 asking the court to adjourn the possession proceedings. She demonstrates that she has recently secured a new job and can meet current mortgage payments and clear the arrears within 18 months. The trial judge adjourns the proceedings for 12 months. Fenwick appeals, arguing the judge had no power to go beyond a short adjournment.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the court's power and whether Fenwick's appeal is likely to succeed?

options.A: Under the AJA 1970, the court may adjourn or suspend a possession order if it appears likely the mortgagor can pay the sums due within a reasonable period; what is reasonable depends on the mortgagor's realistic ability to pay.
options.B: The court may only postpone possession for a maximum period of two years under the AJA 1970; a 12-month adjournment is within this limit, so Fenwick's appeal will fail.
options.C: The court has no power to adjourn possession proceedings once a lender has satisfied the s 103 LPA 1925 criteria; Fenwick's appeal will succeed.
options.D: Oona can only rely on AJA 1970 protections if she is in arrears for no more than three months; since she has been in arrears for 18 months, the court had no power to adjourn and Fenwick's appeal will succeed.
options.E: The court has an absolute discretion to adjourn possession proceedings indefinitely where the mortgagor occupies the property as a home; Fenwick's appeal will fail.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under s 36 Administration of Justice Act 1970 (as amended by s 8 AJA 1973), where the mortgaged property is a dwelling house, the court may adjourn proceedings, or stay or suspend a possession order, if it appears likely that the mortgagor will be able within a 'reasonable period' to pay any sums due under the mortgage or remedy a default. The House of Lords in Cheltenham and Gloucester Building Society v Norgan established that courts should consider what period is needed to pay the whole outstanding debt, not just arrears — 18 months is not inherently unreasonable if the borrower can demonstrate credible repayment. Fenwick's appeal is unlikely to succeed if the judge was satisfied by Oona's evidence. C: Incorrect — the AJA 1970 expressly grants courts the power to adjourn, independent of whether s 103 criteria are met. D: Incorrect — there is no statutory two-year maximum. E: Incorrect — the discretion is not absolute and indefinite; it must be linked to a realistic prospect of repayment. B: Incorrect — there is no three-month arrears limit on the court's jurisdiction under AJA 1970.

questions.scenario: Blackwood Estates Ltd owns a large mixed-use development. It sells Plot A to Reza, a residential buyer, granting him the right to use an ornamental garden maintained by Blackwood's on the adjoining Plot B for recreational purposes. Blackwood's retains Plot B. At the same time, Blackwood's grants Reza 'the right to require Blackwood's to maintain the garden in good order at all times.' Reza also claims that the benefit of a public footpath crossing Plot B should be treated as an easement in his favour.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best analyses the status of all three rights claimed by Reza?

options.A: The right to use the garden is not a valid easement because it amounts to exclusive possession of Plot B; the maintenance obligation is valid; the public footpath becomes private once Reza buys Plot A.
options.B: All three rights are valid easements: the garden use right satisfies Re Ellenborough Park; the maintenance obligation is a valid easement because it is annexed to land; and the public footpath becomes a private easement when Plot A is sold.
options.C: The right to use the garden is a valid easement (Re Ellenborough Park)
options.D: The garden right is a valid easement; the maintenance obligation is valid because it touches and concerns the land; the public footpath is an incorporeal hereditament that passes to Reza under s 62 LPA 1925.
options.E: None of the three rights are easements: recreational use was rejected in Re Ellenborough Park; the maintenance obligation fails because it is not granted by deed; and a public footpath cannot be an easement.

questions.explanation: C is correct. On the garden right: Re Ellenborough Park confirmed that the right to use an ornamental communal garden for recreational purposes satisfies the four capability criteria — there is a dominant and servient tenement, the right accommodates the dominant tenement (it enhances the residential use of Plot A), there is diversity of ownership, and the right lies in grant. This is a valid easement. On the maintenance obligation: an easement cannot impose a positive financial burden on the servient owner (Jones v Pritchard; Regis Property v Redman). A right requiring Blackwood's to maintain the garden at its own expense imposes exactly this and is disqualified. On the public footpath: a public right is exercised by the general public at large, not in favour of a particular dominant tenement — it cannot be an easement in Reza's favour (W3 notes: public rights are distinct from easements). E: Incorrect — maintenance obligations cannot be easements. A: Incorrect — the garden right does not amount to exclusive possession; Re Ellenborough Park itself upheld this type of shared use. B: Incorrect — Re Ellenborough Park upheld recreational easements; it did not reject them. D: Incorrect — a public footpath is not an incorporeal hereditament benefiting Plot A; it is a right of the public at large and cannot pass under s 62 as a private easement.

questions.scenario: For 22 years, Ingrid has driven her tractor across a strip of farmland owned by her neighbour, Caspar, to access the rear field of her farm. Ingrid has never asked Caspar for permission, and Caspar has never objected. However, Ingrid only uses the track during harvest season — approximately six to eight weeks each year — and during certain winters does not use it at all if the ground is too frozen. Caspar argues that no easement has arisen because use has been too infrequent. Ingrid argues that the use has been without force, secrecy or permission.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Ingrid and Caspar on whether a prescriptive easement has arisen?

options.A: A prescriptive easement has arisen automatically after 20 years under the Prescription Act 1832 because no express grant can be traced.
options.B: A prescriptive easement has likely arisen under the lost modern grant doctrine: Ingrid has used the path openly, without force or permission (nec vi, nec clam, nec precario) for over 20 years.
options.C: No prescriptive easement has arisen because Ingrid has not used the track continuously for 20 years without any interruption, including winters where she did not use it at all.
options.D: Ingrid can only claim a prescriptive easement under the Lost Modern Grant doctrine if she can prove the original grant document was destroyed.
options.E: No prescriptive easement has arisen because Caspar's failure to object constitutes permission, which defeats the nec precario requirement.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The basic criterion for prescription is continuous user as of right (nec vi, nec clam, nec precario). 'Continuous' means 'reasonably regular' given the nature of the right — it does not require literally unbroken daily use. In Mills v Silver, use of a farm track whenever it was dry enough, over many decades, was held sufficient even though use was intermittent. Seasonal use of a farm track during harvest is exactly the kind of use that is 'reasonably regular' given the nature of the right. The use is without force (no barriers ignored, no protests overridden), without secrecy (open and visible), and without permission (Caspar never granted permission — mere toleration of user without objection is not 'permission'). C: Incorrect — the law does not require literally uninterrupted use; 'reasonably regular' use appropriate to the character of the right is sufficient. E: Incorrect — Caspar's silence and inaction is toleration, not permission; W3 notes confirm that toleration of user is not 'permission' for prescription purposes. A: Incorrect — the Prescription Act 1832 requires at least 20 years' use but does not operate automatically; it still requires the nec vi, nec clam, nec precario conditions, and a claim must be asserted. D: Incorrect — Lost Modern Grant does not require proof that a document was destroyed; it is a legal fiction that a grant was made and lost.

questions.tag: Implied Acquisition — Wheeldon v Burrows and s 62 LPA 1925
questions.scenario: Miroslava owns a large plot. She uses a path across the southern section to reach the northern section daily, and parks her car on a section of the southern plot adjacent to the path. She sells the northern section to Callum by a transfer deed that makes no mention of the path or the parking. Shortly afterwards, Miroslava sells the southern section to a third party, Yasmin. Yasmin blocks the path and the parking area. Callum claims implied easements over both. The transfer deed to Callum is a legal deed but there was never any prior separate occupation of the northern and southern plots.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best analyses Callum's claims to implied easements under Wheeldon v Burrows and/or s 62 LPA 1925?

options.A: Callum can rely only on s 62 LPA 1925 for the path and parking, as Wheeldon v Burrows requires the easement to be absolutely necessary for the use of the dominant land, which is a higher threshold than the one satisfied here.
options.B: Callum can claim both rights under Wheeldon v Burrows as quasi-easements that were continuous, apparent, and reasonably necessary for enjoyment; he cannot rely on s 62 LPA 1925 because there was no prior diversity of occupation.
options.C: Callum can rely on s 62 LPA 1925 to upgrade the parking licence into a legal easement because there was diversity of occupation between Miroslava and Callum after the transfer; he cannot rely on Wheeldon v Burrows for the parking as it is not continuous and apparent.
options.D: Callum cannot claim any implied easement because the transfer deed is silent and courts will not imply rights that the parties could have included expressly.
options.E: Callum can claim both the path and parking as implied easements under Wheeldon v Burrows because both were in use by Miroslava before the transfer and are reasonably necessary for the use of the northern plot.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Wheeldon v Burrows, an easement can be implied on a grant if at the time of the transfer the right was: (i) continuous and apparent; (ii) necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the land granted; and (iii) in use by the common owner at the date of transfer. A daily-used path and adjacent parking area both satisfy these requirements — they were quasi-easements in Miroslava's use, they are reasonably necessary for reasonable enjoyment, and they were in use at the date of transfer. The implied easements take their status from the document into which they are implied — a legal deed — so they are legal implied easements. On s 62: the 'upgrade' effect of s 62 requires prior diversity of occupation (Wright v Macadam), which is absent here. However, P&S Platt v Crouch and Wood v Waddington confirm that where a right is 'continuous and apparent', s 62 can also imply it even without prior diversity. Either way, Wheeldon v Burrows is the clearest route here. E: Incorrect — identifies the right outcome for Wheeldon v Burrows but fails to address s 62 limitations or explain the legal status. A: Incorrect — Wheeldon v Burrows does not require absolute necessity; 'necessary for reasonable enjoyment' is a lower threshold. D: Incorrect — implied acquisition doctrines exist precisely to fill gaps in transfer deeds. C: Incorrect — there was no diversity of occupation before the transfer to Callum; the upgrade effect of s 62 in the diversity sense is not available, though P&S Platt offers an alternative route.

questions.scenario: Thirteen years ago, Florence was granted an equitable easement of drainage over her neighbour's registered freehold land by a written agreement signed by both parties. The easement was never protected by notice on the charges register of the servient land. The servient owner recently sold the land to Priya, who paid full market value. Florence has now discovered that Priya intends to block the drainage pipes. Florence also believes she may have an implied legal easement of way across the same land, which she has exercised openly every week for the last 13 years.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Florence on the enforceability of both the equitable easement and the implied legal easement against Priya?

options.A: The equitable easement binds Priya because she had actual notice of Florence's drainage system; the implied legal easement is not binding because it was not registered under s 27 LRA 2002.
options.B: The equitable easement of drainage is not binding on Priya because it was not protected by notice on the charges register under s 32 LRA 2002, and Priya as a purchaser for value takes free of it under s 29(1) LRA 2002
options.C: Both easements are binding on Priya because she had constructive notice of Florence's use of the servient land, and LRA 2002 preserves all existing easements.
options.D: The equitable easement is binding on Priya under the basic rule (s 28 LRA 2002) because it pre-dates Priya's purchase; the implied legal easement binds Priya automatically as a legal interest.
options.E: Neither easement is binding on Priya because neither has been substantively registered under s 27 LRA 2002.

questions.explanation: B is correct. On the equitable easement: under s 29 LRA 2002, a registrable disposition (including a transfer of registered freehold) for valuable consideration takes priority over any pre-existing interests except those that are protected on the register or are overriding interests. An equitable easement must be protected by notice in the charges register (s 32 LRA 2002). Because Florence did not protect the easement, Priya as a purchaser for value is not bound (s 29(1)). On the implied legal easement: an implied legal easement over registered land can be an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 3 LRA 2002 if: (a) it is within Priya's actual knowledge; or (b) it is obvious on a reasonably careful inspection; or (c) it has been exercised within one year of the transfer. Florence has used the right of way openly every week for 13 years — it has clearly been exercised within one year of the transfer and is likely obvious on inspection. The easement overrides the transfer and binds Priya. C: Incorrect — the concept of constructive notice does not apply to registered land in the same way; the LRA 2002 replaces notice with registration. E: Incorrect — the s 28 basic rule is displaced by s 29 where a purchaser for value is involved. D: Incorrect — implied legal easements do not require substantive registration; they can be overriding interests under Sch 3 para 3. A: Incorrect — actual notice alone does not protect an unregistered equitable easement against a purchaser for value under LRA 2002.

questions.scenario: Clarence owns a small car park with ten marked bays adjacent to his industrial unit. He sells Plot X, an industrial unit, to Deirdre, and grants her the right to use all ten marked bays in the car park at all times of the day for the parking of vehicles used in her business. Clarence himself has no operational vehicles. He argues the right is not an easement because it effectively ousts him from his own land. Deirdre's solicitor argues that Clarence can still walk across the car park and use it for other non-vehicular purposes.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best analyses whether the parking right is capable of being an easement?

options.A: The right cannot be an easement because Deirdre's industrial unit is not adjacent to the car park, and the dominant and servient tenements must be adjoining.
options.B: The right to use all ten bays is capable of being an easement because Moncrieff v Jamieson establishes that the fact the servient owner has no practical use of the land himself is irrelevant provided he retains ultimate possession and control.
options.C: The right to use all ten bays at all times cannot be an easement because it leaves Clarence with no reasonable use of his car park, satisfying the ouster principle from Batchelor v Marlow.
options.D: The right to park in all ten bays for all times is capable of being an easement if it satisfies Re Ellenborough Park — but exclusive use of all bays at all times may amount to ownership rather than an easement.
options.E: Parking rights can never be easements because a right to park amounts to exclusive possession in all circumstances.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The capability of a parking right to be an easement is established (Kettel v Bloomfold; Moncrieff v Jamieson). The key question here is whether the grant of all ten bays at all times ousts Clarence from reasonable use of his land. The ouster principle from Batchelor v Marlow (binding English authority) asks whether the servient owner is left with no 'reasonable use' of the land. If Clarence has no vehicles and the right covers all bays at all times, this is a strong argument that he retains no reasonable use. However, the possession and control test from Moncrieff (Scottish, persuasive only) asks whether Clarence retains ultimate possession and control. Kettel v Bloomfold applied Batchelor in a 'Moncrieff way', suggesting that provided the servient owner can do anything except interfere with the parking, the right will not be disqualified. This is an uncertain area and the outcome depends on application of binding English authority (Batchelor). C: Incorrect — while Batchelor may well apply, it is not inevitable that all ten bays for all times satisfies the ouster test; the analysis must be applied to the facts. B: Incorrect — Moncrieff is Scottish and persuasive only, not binding English law. E: Incorrect — parking rights have been recognised as capable of being easements. A: Incorrect — the dominant and servient tenements do not need to adjoin, only to be sufficiently proximate (Pugh v Savage).

questions.scenario: Groundwork Developments Ltd sells a plot of land to Stefan with the benefit of planning permission for the construction of a single residential dwelling. The transfer deed makes no mention of any rights to connect to Groundwork's neighbouring land for electricity, water, or sewage. Stefan begins construction and discovers that the only feasible route for utility connections crosses Groundwork's retained land. Groundwork refuses to grant any access and argues that Stefan knew about this issue before purchase and chose not to raise it.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Stefan on whether an easement for utility connections can be implied by common intention?

options.A: Stefan can claim an implied easement by common intention only if he can prove that Groundwork expressly stated before the sale that utilities would be available across its land.
options.B: Stefan cannot claim an implied easement because he knew about the issue before purchase and failed to raise it expressly; the duty to reserve or secure rights lies with the buyer.
options.C: Stefan can claim an implied easement of necessity for the utility connections because without them his land is effectively landlocked.
options.D: Stefan can claim an implied easement by common intention — both parties intended the land to be used as a residential building plot, which necessarily requires utility connections, giving rise to an implied easement of necessity.
options.E: Stefan has no claim for any implied easement because implied easements by common intention can only arise in favour of the seller, not the buyer.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Implied acquisition by common intention requires that land was sold for a particular purpose and that purpose cannot be achieved without the easement claimed. Both parties' common intention must be specific, not merely general (Pwllbach Colliery v Woodman). Here, the plot was sold with planning permission for a single residential dwelling — this is a specific intention that the land is to be used as a building plot. Donovan v Rana held that where a plot was sold with planning permission for a dwelling, an easement for services (electricity, sewage) was implied by common intention because the parties clearly intended the plot to be developed. Utility connections are necessary to achieve that specific purpose. E: Incorrect — the duty to raise matters expressly goes to express easements; implied easements by common intention can supply what the parties mutually intended even where not expressly agreed. C: Incorrect — implied by necessity only applies where the dominant land would be completely unusable without the easement — i.e. it is typically confined to the right of way to landlocked land (Pryce v McGuinness); utility connections are not absolute necessities to the same degree. B: Incorrect — common intention is assessed objectively from the circumstances, not by proving an express pre-sale representation. A: Incorrect — implied by common intention operates in favour of grantees (buyers/tenants); it can also operate in favour of the grantor in reservation cases, but is available to Stefan here.

questions.tag: Passing the Burden in Equity — Tulk v Moxhay
questions.scenario: In 2005, Francesca sold the rear portion of her garden to Nikesh. The transfer deed contained a covenant by Nikesh that he would not erect any building on the land exceeding one storey, and a second covenant that he would maintain the shared boundary fence at his own expense. Both covenants were protected by notice on Nikesh's title at the Land Registry. Nikesh later sold to Oskar, who began constructing a two-storey extension and allowed the fence to fall into disrepair. Francesca's successor, Ingrid, now seeks to enforce both covenants against Oskar.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Ingrid on the enforceability of both covenants against Oskar?

options.A: The fence maintenance covenant is enforceable against Oskar in equity as a mixed covenant with both positive and negative elements; the height restriction covenant fails because it was not entered into by deed.
options.B: Neither covenant is enforceable against Oskar because the burden of covenants between freeholders cannot pass to successors under any circumstances.
options.C: The height restriction covenant is enforceable against Oskar under Tulk v Moxhay because it is restrictive in nature, was intended to run with the land, touches and concerns the land, and Oskar took with notice.
options.D: Both covenants are enforceable against Oskar at common law because they were entered into by a deed and both touch and concern the land.
options.E: Both covenants are enforceable against Oskar in equity because they were both protected by notice on the register and both touch and concern the land.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under the Tulk v Moxhay equitable rules, the burden of a restrictive covenant passes to a successor covenantor if: (1) the covenant is restrictive; (2) it accommodates the dominant tenement (touches and concerns the land); (3) there is intention for the burden to run; and (4) the successor has notice (by registration). The height restriction is restrictive — Nikesh promised not to erect buildings exceeding one storey; it touches and concerns the land (affects its use and value), there is implied intention under s 79 LPA 1925, and it is protected by notice. All four Tulk v Moxhay requirements are satisfied. However, the fence maintenance covenant is positive — it requires Nikesh to spend money and effort maintaining the fence (the 'hand in pocket' test from Haywood v Brunswick). The burden of positive covenants does not pass to successors in equity (Rhone v Stephens). Ingrid's remedies against Oskar for the fence are limited to pursuing the original covenantor (Nikesh) in damages, subject to an indemnity chain. E: Incorrect — notice on the register does not render positive covenants enforceable in equity against successors. B: Incorrect — the burden of restrictive covenants can pass in equity under Tulk v Moxhay. D: Incorrect — at common law, the burden of covenants does not pass to successors (Austerberry v Oldham; Rhone v Stephens). A: Incorrect — while mixed covenants can be split, the fence covenant in its substance requires expenditure and is positive in nature.

questions.scenario: In 1998, Quentin sold land to Rowena, who entered into a restrictive covenant not to use the land for commercial purposes. The deed of transfer stated that the benefit of the covenant was for 'the benefit of Quentin's retained land and each and every part thereof'. The restrictive covenant was protected by notice. Quentin later sold his retained land to Stefanie without expressly mentioning or assigning the covenant. Stefanie now seeks to enforce the covenant against Rowena's successor, Tomasz.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best analyses whether Stefanie can enforce the covenant against Tomasz?

options.A: Stefanie can only enforce the covenant if she can show she acquired the benefit by express assignment from Quentin and gave notice of the assignment to Tomasz.
options.B: The benefit of the covenant has not passed to Stefanie because the original deed did not refer to 's 78 LPA 1925' expressly, and annexation requires such a reference.
options.C: Stefanie cannot enforce the covenant against Tomasz because the benefit of restrictive covenants must be expressly assigned each time the dominant land is sold.
options.D: Stefanie can enforce the covenant at common law because the benefit of restrictive covenants passes automatically with the land.
options.E: Stefanie can enforce the covenant against Tomasz because the benefit was annexed under s 78 LPA 1925 and passes automatically to successors; the burden has run to Tomasz as a restrictive covenant protected on the register.

questions.explanation: E is correct. The benefit of a restrictive covenant can pass to successors in equity by annexation, express assignment, or building scheme. Here, the deed expressly stated the covenant was for the benefit of 'Quentin's retained land and each and every part thereof', which satisfies the requirement for express annexation. Furthermore, s 78 LPA 1925 (as interpreted in Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties) provides that a covenant relating to land is deemed to be made with the covenantee and his successors in title — this means annexation is automatic in all but the most exceptional circumstances, without needing an express reference to s 78 in the deed. Since the benefit was annexed to the dominant land, it passes automatically to each successive owner (Stefanie) without express assignment. Tomasz as successor covenantor is bound because the covenant is protected by notice under s 32 LRA 2002. B: Incorrect — this describes express assignment, which is one of three routes; annexation makes express assignment unnecessary. A: Incorrect — notice of assignment is a requirement for express assignment under s 136 LPA 1925, not for annexation. D: Incorrect — the burden of restrictive covenants does not pass at common law; this analysis concerns the benefit. C: Incorrect — Federated Homes established that s 78 operates automatically; there is no requirement to cite s 78 expressly in the deed.

questions.tag: Doctrine of Mutual Benefit and Burden
questions.scenario: Harrington Estates Ltd sells units on an estate to various buyers. Each buyer receives the benefit of a right of way over Harrington's private roads and, in the same transfer, covenants to contribute one-fifth of the cost of maintaining those roads. Harrington sells its retained land to Meridian Investments Ltd, which decides not to maintain the roads at all. One buyer, Anika, refuses to pay the maintenance contribution on the basis that Meridian is no longer maintaining the roads and that, as a successor covenantor, she cannot be bound by the positive maintenance covenant.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on Anika's position and on whether she can be bound by the positive covenant?

options.A: Anika is bound by the positive covenant because it was made by deed and touched and concerned the land, satisfying common law requirements for the passing of the burden.
options.B: Anika is bound by the positive covenant because it was protected by notice on her title at the Land Registry, which makes all covenants binding on successors.
options.C: Anika cannot be bound by the positive maintenance covenant because the burden of positive covenants never passes to successors in equity or at common law.
options.D: Anika is bound by the positive covenant under the doctrine of mutual benefit and burden: she takes the benefit of the right of way and cannot do so without accepting the connected maintenance obligation.
options.E: Anika is not bound because the benefit and burden are not sufficiently connected — the right of way and the maintenance obligation are separate legal relationships.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The doctrine of mutual benefit and burden (Halsall v Brizell; confirmed in Rhone v Stephens) provides a limited exception to the general rule that positive covenant burdens do not pass to successors. Three conditions must be met: (1) there must be a clear link between the benefit and burden — the right of way and the maintenance contribution are clearly and directly connected; (2) there must be a genuine choice — Anika could in theory relinquish the right of way and escape the burden; (3) the benefit and burden must have been conferred in the same transaction — they were, in the same transfer deed. Anika therefore cannot enjoy the right of way while refusing to pay the maintenance contribution. If she genuinely relinquishes the right of way, she could avoid the burden (Thamesmead Town Ltd v Allotey). A: Incorrect — the mutual benefit and burden doctrine is an established exception to this general rule. B: Incorrect — notice on the register protects restrictive covenants in equity; it does not independently bind successors to positive covenants. E: Incorrect — the link between road access and road maintenance is sufficiently direct and clear (Davies v Jones; the benefit and burden were in the same transaction). C: Incorrect — at common law the burden of covenants (positive or negative) does not pass to successors; the analysis applies in equity via mutual benefit and burden.

questions.tag: Discharge and Modification under s 84 LPA 1925
questions.scenario: A restrictive covenant was imposed in 1963, restricting a plot of land to residential use only. The surrounding area has since been comprehensively redeveloped and is now a busy commercial district; the plot is the only remaining residential plot in the area, surrounded by offices and retail units on all sides. The owner, Celestine, applies to the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s 84 LPA 1925 for discharge of the covenant. The dominant owner, who purchased the adjacent land with the benefit of the covenant in 2019, objects and threatens to seek an injunction.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Celestine on her application and the dominant owner's threatened injunction?

options.A: Celestine cannot apply for discharge under s 84 LPA 1925 because the covenant was imposed more than 50 years ago and is therefore automatically extinguished.
options.B: Celestine has strong grounds under s 84(1)(a) LPA 1925 (obsolescence due to changes in neighbourhood character) and s 84(1)(aa) (the covenant impedes reasonable use of the land without practical benefit to those entitled to enforce it).
options.C: Celestine cannot apply under s 84 because the covenant was privately agreed between the original parties and the Tribunal has no jurisdiction over privately negotiated covenants.
options.D: Celestine can apply under s 84 LPA 1925, but s 84 only applies to positive covenants; since this is a restrictive covenant, only the court can discharge it.
options.E: The dominant owner can successfully injunct Celestine from breaching the covenant regardless of the Tribunal application, and the Tribunal has no power to override an existing court injunction.

questions.explanation: B is correct. S 84(1) LPA 1925 grants the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) the power to wholly or partially discharge or modify any restrictive covenant on a number of grounds. S 84(1)(a) applies where the covenant has become obsolete due to changes in the character of the property or the neighbourhood — the comprehensive commercial redevelopment of the surrounding area strongly supports this. S 84(1)(aa) applies where the continued existence of the covenant impedes the reasonable use of the land for public or private purposes; the Tribunal must be satisfied that the covenant either confers no practical benefit of substantial value, or that its restriction is contrary to public interest, and that financial compensation would adequately compensate the dominant owner. The Tribunal may discharge the covenant and award compensation. An injunction obtained by the dominant owner before discharge would be relevant to the timing of any breach, but the Tribunal's power is independent of the courts. A: Incorrect — there is no time limit after which a covenant is automatically extinguished. E: Incorrect — the Tribunal's power is independent; a s 84 discharge, if granted, would be a complete defence to any enforcement claim. D: Incorrect — s 84 LPA 1925 explicitly applies to restrictive covenants, not positive covenants. C: Incorrect — s 84 applies to all restrictive covenants over land, regardless of whether they were privately negotiated.

questions.scenario: Annalise has lived in her partner Xavier's registered freehold property for six years. Xavier holds the legal title alone. Annalise has contributed to mortgage repayments and carried out significant renovation works, giving her a beneficial interest under a constructive trust. Xavier, without Annalise's knowledge, mortgages the property to Fenton Building Society by a legal mortgage created by deed and registered. Fenton conducts no inspection of the property before registering the mortgage and does not ask Xavier about occupiers. Fenton now seeks possession following Xavier's default.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Annalise on whether her beneficial interest is binding on Fenton?

options.A: Annalise's beneficial interest is automatically binding on Fenton regardless of registration or occupation because Fenton had constructive notice of her presence.
options.B: Annalise's beneficial interest cannot bind Fenton because beneficial interests in trusts of land must be protected by restriction on the register to bind a mortgagee, and no restriction was entered.
options.C: Annalise's beneficial interest cannot bind Fenton because overriding interests under Sch 3 para 2 only protect short legal leases, not equitable interests in trusts.
options.D: Annalise's beneficial interest may bind Fenton as an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002, provided she was in actual occupation at the date of the disposition and the interest was not disclosable on a reasonably careful inspection.
options.E: Annalise's interest is binding on Fenton only if she registered a restriction on the register before the mortgage was completed.

questions.explanation: D is correct. A beneficial interest under a constructive trust cannot be protected by notice (s 33 LRA 2002); it must be protected by restriction. If no restriction was entered, the interest would not be protected by entry. However, a person in actual occupation may have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002. For this: (1) Annalise must have an interest (she does — constructive trust); (2) she must be in actual occupation; (3) her interest must not have been excluded by disclosure on enquiry; and (4) it must either be within the actual knowledge of the disponee, or be obvious on a reasonably careful inspection. Since Fenton conducted no inspection and did not enquire about occupiers, it cannot rely on the 'obvious on inspection' exception to escape the overriding interest — Fenton took the risk of not inspecting. Annalise's interest is therefore likely binding as an overriding interest. B: Incorrect — a restriction is the appropriate protection for beneficial interests, but failure to register a restriction does not automatically defeat an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2. A: Incorrect — the doctrine of constructive notice does not apply to registered land transactions; the LRA 2002 framework governs. C: Incorrect — Sch 3 para 2 applies to persons in actual occupation, including those with equitable interests under trusts. E: Incorrect — a restriction may have been appropriate, but actual occupation provides an alternative route to binding a disponee.

questions.scenario: Bramble House is a registered freehold property held on trust by two trustees, Conrad and Diana, for the benefit of themselves and their adult daughter, Eleanor. Conrad and Diana decide to sell the property to Yusuf for £600,000. Yusuf pays the full purchase price to Conrad alone, after Diana had a falling-out with Conrad and refused to participate. The TR1 transfer form is signed only by Conrad. Eleanor, who lives in the property, was not told about the sale. Eleanor claims her beneficial interest is binding on Yusuf.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Yusuf on whether Eleanor's interest has been overreached and whether he takes the property free of it?

options.A: Eleanor's interest has not been overreached because the purchase price was not paid to at least two trustees as required by ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925
options.B: Eleanor's interest has been overreached because Conrad had authority to bind Diana as co-trustee, and Yusuf acted in good faith.
options.C: Yusuf takes free of Eleanor's interest because Eleanor failed to enter a restriction on the register to protect her beneficial interest, and failure to protect means the interest is void against a purchaser.
options.D: Eleanor's interest is binding on Yusuf regardless of overreaching because she is in actual occupation and actual occupation always defeats a purchaser.
options.E: Yusuf takes the property free of Eleanor's interest because the purchase price was paid to Conrad, who is a trustee, and one trustee is sufficient to overreach a beneficial interest.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Overreaching under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925 requires that the purchase price (capital money) be paid to at least two trustees. This is an absolute requirement — payment to one trustee is insufficient. Since Yusuf paid only Conrad, overreaching has not occurred. Eleanor's beneficial interest therefore remains attached to the land rather than shifting to the proceeds. Eleanor may then have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002 if she was in actual occupation, her interest was not excluded by disclosure on enquiry, and it was either within Yusuf's knowledge or obvious on inspection. Since Eleanor lived in the property, actual occupation is likely satisfied. Yusuf cannot take free unless he can show he had no actual knowledge and the interest was not obvious on a reasonably careful inspection. E: Incorrect — the statutory requirement of payment to two trustees cannot be satisfied by payment to one alone. B: Incorrect — Conrad cannot bind Diana unilaterally as a co-trustee for these purposes; the two-trustee rule is mandatory. C: Incorrect — failure to protect by restriction does not automatically defeat an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2; it means the interest cannot be protected by restriction but actual occupation provides an alternative. D: Incorrect — actual occupation does not automatically defeat a purchaser in all circumstances; the Sch 3 para 2 conditions must be satisfied.

questions.tag: Unregistered Land — Doctrine of Notice and Land Charges
questions.scenario: In 1985, Harriet was granted an equitable easement of way over unregistered land owned by her neighbour, Vincent, evidenced by a written agreement signed by both parties. Harriet did not register a land charge. In 2002, Vincent sold the land to William, a purchaser for value who had no knowledge of the easement but noticed the worn path on inspection. William now sells to Petra, who carries out a search of the Land Charges Register against the name 'Vincent E. Smithson'. The title deeds spelt his name as 'Vincent Ernest Smithson'. Harriet wishes to enforce the easement against Petra.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Harriet on whether her easement is enforceable against Petra?

options.A: Harriet's easement was void against William as a purchaser for value under s 4(6) LCA 1972 because it was unregistered; it cannot be revived against Petra who derives title from William.
options.B: Harriet's easement is enforceable against Petra because Petra purchased from William, not from the original owner, and she must be bound by all rights that bound William.
options.C: Harriet's easement is enforceable against Petra only if she registers it as a Class D(III) land charge before Petra completes the purchase.
options.D: Harriet's easement is enforceable against Petra because the land charge search was conducted against an incorrect name, so Petra had constructive notice of the easement.
options.E: Harriet's easement is enforceable against Petra because Petra's solicitor had constructive notice of the path on inspection.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under s 4(6) LCA 1972, an equitable easement created after 1926 over unregistered land (which falls under Class D(III)) is void against a purchaser for money or money's worth of a legal estate if it is not registered as a land charge. This is absolute — knowledge of the interest is irrelevant (Midland Bank Trust Co Ltd v Green). William was a purchaser for value of a legal estate and the easement was not protected by land charge. The easement was therefore void against William. Once an equitable interest is defeated by equity's darling (a bona fide purchaser, or in the unregistered land context, a purchaser for value), the interest is extinguished and cannot be revived against any subsequent purchaser (including Petra), even one with notice. The search error against Petra is therefore irrelevant — there is nothing left to protect. E: Incorrect — constructive notice is irrelevant in the land charges context; s 198 LPA 1925 makes registration (or non-registration) conclusive. D: Incorrect — the search error may give Petra no protection against a validly registered charge, but here the interest was already destroyed. B: Incorrect — Petra takes the land as William sold it; since William took free of the easement, there is no easement for Petra to be bound by. C: Incorrect — the time for registration has passed; the interest was already destroyed when William purchased.

questions.tag: Overriding Interests — Schedule 1 and First Registration
questions.scenario: Elspeth has used a path across a strip of unregistered land owned by Gordon for 25 years, openly and without permission, as an access route to her own land. The path is clearly visible on the land. Gordon recently sold the land to Hamish, who was required to register the land for the first time with the Land Registry. Hamish's solicitor failed to note the path on the application for first registration. Hamish immediately blocks the path. Elspeth claims a prescriptive easement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Elspeth on whether her prescriptive easement is binding on Hamish following first registration?

options.A: Elspeth's easement is only binding on Hamish if it can be shown that Hamish had actual knowledge of the path at the time of first registration.
options.B: Elspeth's prescriptive legal easement is a legal interest in unregistered land that binds the world ('legal interests bind the world')
options.C: Elspeth cannot claim a prescriptive easement because the land was unregistered and prescription can only arise over registered land.
options.D: Elspeth's easement is not binding on Hamish because it was not noted on the register at first registration, and all interests must be on the register to bind a new owner.
options.E: Elspeth's easement is binding on Hamish only if she registers it as a Class D(III) land charge before first registration is complete.

questions.explanation: B is correct. A prescriptive easement acquired over unregistered land is a legal easement. Legal easements over unregistered land bind the world ('legal interests bind the world', confirmed in Mercer v Liverpool). On first registration of the servient land (Sch 1 LRA 2002), certain interests override first registration without needing to be noted. Sch 1 para 3 LRA 2002 provides that a legal easement or profit à prendre that was an overriding interest before first registration continues to bind the land after registration without any requirement for it to be noted. Elspeth's 25-year prescriptive legal easement was binding on Gordon before sale, and it binds Hamish as an overriding interest on first registration. D: Incorrect — Sch 1 para 3 specifically protects existing legal easements on first registration, regardless of whether they are noted. A: Incorrect — Sch 1 para 3 does not impose a knowledge requirement unlike Sch 3 para 3 (which applies to subsequent registered dispositions). C: Incorrect — prescription can arise over both unregistered and registered land. E: Incorrect — equitable easements are registered as Class D(III); a legal easement binds the world and does not require land charge registration.

questions.scenario: Philippa and Quentin purchased a freehold property as joint tenants in both law and equity. Three years later, they separate. Quentin writes and sends a letter to Philippa's last known address stating: 'I hereby sever the joint tenancy.' The letter is returned undelivered. Philippa later dies, having made a will leaving all her property to her sister Rowena. Quentin asserts that severance was not effective and that by survivorship he is now the sole owner. Rowena asserts she inherits Philippa's share under the will.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether the joint tenancy was severed and who owns the property?

options.A: The joint tenancy was not severed because severance requires a deed; Quentin now owns the entire property by survivorship.
options.B: The joint tenancy was severed because Quentin's letter demonstrated a unilateral intention to sever; Rowena inherits Philippa's 50% share under her will.
options.C: The joint tenancy was automatically severed when Philippa and Quentin separated, and Rowena is therefore entitled to Philippa's 50% share.
options.D: The joint tenancy was validly severed by Quentin's letter; survivorship cannot operate once a notice of severance has been posted, regardless of whether it is received.
options.E: The joint tenancy was not severed because a valid notice under s 196(4) LPA 1925 must be delivered to the last-known place of abode or business; delivery to the wrong address is ineffective.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A joint tenancy can be severed by written notice under s 196(3) LPA 1925. Under s 196(4), such a notice is sufficiently served if it is left at the last-known place of abode or business of the person to be served. The courts have interpreted s 196 as requiring proper delivery to the correct address — it does not require actual receipt provided it reaches the correct address (Kinch v Bullard). However, if the letter was sent to a wrong address and returned, it may not have been properly served. The key factual issue is whether 'last known address' was correctly used. On the assumption the address was correct, delivery to that address is sufficient even if the addressee does not in fact receive it. If incorrectly addressed, severance fails. On the facts stated, the outcome is uncertain — it turns on whether the address used was Philippa's last-known place of abode. B: Incorrect — unilateral intention alone is not enough; s 196 requires proper service. A: Incorrect — separation alone does not sever a joint tenancy; an act of severance is required. C: Incorrect — posting is not sufficient; service under s 196(4) requires delivery to the last-known place of abode or business, not mere posting. D: Incorrect — a deed is not required for severance by notice; a written notice delivered in accordance with s 196 is sufficient.

questions.tag: TOLATA — Dispute Resolution and s 15 Factors
questions.scenario: Marcus and Natasha are co-owners of a family home registered in both their names as beneficial joint tenants. They have two children aged 8 and 11. Marcus becomes insolvent and his trustee in bankruptcy, appointed two years ago, applies to the court under TOLATA 1996 s 14 for an order for sale of the property. The family home was purchased specifically as a home for the family. Natasha argues strongly against a sale on the basis of the children's welfare. The trustee points out that the debt is rising at £2,000 per month and there is no prospect of recovery.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises the parties on the likely outcome of the trustee in bankruptcy's application?

options.A: The court must refuse sale because the family home purpose of the trust is still continuing and this is the most important s 15 factor.
options.B: The court will refuse sale because the trustee in bankruptcy was appointed over two years ago and has left it too late to make the application; laches applies.
options.C: The court must refuse the order for sale because the welfare of the minor children is the paramount consideration under TOLATA s 15, and a sale would disrupt their home.
options.D: The court can only order sale if Natasha agrees, because the majority of beneficiaries by value oppose it.
options.E: In a trustee in bankruptcy application, s 335A IA 1986 applies rather than TOLATA s 15; after one year from the bankruptcy, the court must order sale unless the circumstances are exceptional, which is a very high threshold.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Where a trustee in bankruptcy applies for sale of co-owned property, the governing legislation is Insolvency Act 1986 s 335A, not TOLATA s 15. Under s 335A, in applications made more than one year after the bankruptcy order, the court must assume that the interests of creditors outweigh all other considerations unless the circumstances are 'exceptional'. The cases establish a high bar for 'exceptional' — simply having children or a family home does not suffice (Re Citro). Given the debt is rising significantly every month with no prospect of recovery, these are not exceptional circumstances. Sale will almost certainly be ordered, possibly with a limited postponement to allow the family to relocate. C: Incorrect — the welfare of children is a factor under TOLATA s 15, but it does not apply in bankruptcy cases; even under TOLATA, it is not paramount. A: Incorrect — the continuing family home purpose carries weight under TOLATA s 15 but is overridden by s 335A in bankruptcy cases. D: Incorrect — the court can order sale even where the majority of beneficiaries by value oppose it; the trustee in bankruptcy stands in the shoes of a creditor and has standing to apply. B: Incorrect — there is no limitation period of the kind suggested for trustee in bankruptcy applications; laches is an equitable doctrine that applies in different circumstances.

questions.scenario: Alinta and Brett purchased a house in Brett's sole name. Alinta contributed £40,000 to the deposit from her savings. The purchase price was £200,000. There was no express declaration of trust. Alinta and Brett discussed that the property would be 'theirs together'. Over five years, Alinta renovated the kitchen and bathroom at her own expense (total £15,000), but made no direct mortgage contributions. Brett now seeks to sell the property and argues Alinta has no interest.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Alinta on whether and to what extent she has a beneficial interest in the property?

options.A: Alinta can only rely on proprietary estoppel; a constructive or resulting trust cannot arise because the property is in Brett's sole name.
options.B: Alinta is likely to have a beneficial interest — either a resulting trust proportionate to her £40,000 deposit contribution, or a common intention constructive trust assessed by reference to the whole course of dealing.
options.C: Alinta has no interest because she made no mortgage contributions, which are the only form of contribution courts consider in this context.
options.D: Alinta has a 50% beneficial share because the discussions showed a common intention that the property was 'theirs together', and courts always award an equal share where common intention is established.
options.E: Alinta has no interest because there is no written declaration of trust signed by Brett as required by s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Implied trusts can arise without written formalities (s 53(2) LPA 1925). A resulting trust may arise from Alinta's direct financial contribution of £40,000 to the deposit — this would give her a proportionate share of approximately 20% (£40,000 / £200,000). A constructive trust can arise more broadly where there is a common intention (express or inferred from conduct) that the property is to be shared, and the claimant has acted to their detriment. The discussion that the property would be 'theirs together' and the renovation works may together establish a common intention constructive trust. Quantification of a constructive trust share takes into account the whole course of the parties' dealing (Stack v Dowden; Jones v Kernott), which may include the renovation expenditure. The precise share is uncertain — it is not automatically 50%. D: Incorrect — resulting and constructive trusts are exempt from the writing formalities by s 53(2) LPA 1925. E: Incorrect — courts do not automatically award 50%; quantification depends on all the circumstances. C: Incorrect — courts can consider all financial contributions including deposit payments and renovation works, not only mortgage contributions. A: Incorrect — resulting and constructive trusts are well-established routes even where property is in one party's sole name; proprietary estoppel is a separate but overlapping doctrine.

questions.scenario: Meadow House is registered freehold land held by three trustees: Iris, James, and Kate, on trust for themselves and two minor beneficiaries. Iris and James decide to sell the property to a developer, Oaktree Ltd, for £750,000 without telling Kate. Oaktree pays the full price to Iris and James by bank transfer. Kate and the minor beneficiaries argue their interests are binding on Oaktree.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the effect of the sale on the interests of Kate and the minor beneficiaries?

options.A: Oaktree must take the land subject to all beneficial interests because the trust was imposed by statute and cannot be overreached without a court order.
options.B: Overreaching only applies to registered land where all trustees have participated in the sale; since Kate did not participate, the sale is void.
options.C: The interests of all beneficiaries including Kate are binding on Oaktree because Kate did not consent to the sale, and a trust sale requires all trustee consent.
options.D: Kate's interest is binding on Oaktree as an overriding interest because Kate was in actual occupation of Meadow House; the minor beneficiaries' interests are also overriding.
options.E: Overreaching has occurred because Oaktree paid the purchase price to two trustees (Iris and James), satisfying the minimum two-trustee requirement under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925

questions.explanation: E is correct. Overreaching under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925 requires that capital money be paid to at least two trustees — it does not require the consent or participation of all trustees. Here, payment to Iris and James (two of the three trustees) satisfies this minimum requirement. Overreaching operates automatically once the conditions are met: all beneficial interests (including Kate's and the minor beneficiaries' interests) are detached from the land and attach instead to the proceeds of sale held by Iris and James. Oaktree therefore takes the land free of all beneficial interests. Kate and the minor beneficiaries' remedy lies against Iris and James for breach of trust (or any misappropriation of proceeds), not against Oaktree. C: Incorrect — overreaching does not require all trustees to consent to the sale; payment to two is sufficient. D: Incorrect — once overreaching occurs, it defeats beneficial interests regardless of actual occupation (City of London Building Society v Flegg). B: Incorrect — overreaching applies to registered land; the two-trustee rule is satisfied; Kate's non-participation does not invalidate the sale or prevent overreaching. A: Incorrect — overreaching is a statutory mechanism that operates automatically on payment to two trustees; a court order is not required.

questions.tag: Certainty of Term and Lease vs Licence
questions.scenario: Sylvester is a property developer. He enters into a written agreement with Constance, the owner of a warehouse, described as a 'licence to occupy' at a weekly fee. The agreement permits Sylvester to use the warehouse for storage 'until the planning permission for redevelopment of the site is granted'. Sylvester has exclusive use of the entire warehouse.
