The planning permission has not yet been granted after three years. Constance attempts to revoke the arrangement and remove Sylvester.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Sylvester has a lease or a licence?

options.A: Sylvester has a lease because the court will look at substance over form, and the payment of a weekly fee gives a periodic certainty of term, combined with exclusive possession satisfies both requirements of Street v Mountford.
options.B: Sylvester has a licence because the written agreement expressly uses the words 'licence to occupy' and the court must give effect to the stated intention of the parties.
options.C: Sylvester has a licence because he is a commercial occupier and the courts give greater deference to the label chosen in commercial agreements.
options.D: Sylvester does not have a lease because the arrangement lacks certainty of term — a valid lease requires a definite maximum duration, and tying the end date to an uncertain future event (planning permission) is fatal.
options.E: Sylvester has a lease because he has exclusive possession of the warehouse, which is sufficient on its own to create a lease regardless of any other considerations.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under Street v Mountford, a lease requires certainty of term and exclusive possession. On certainty of term: the arrangement is to continue 'until planning permission is granted' — this is analogous to Lace v Chantler, where a lease 'for the duration of the war' failed for lack of certainty. The timing of planning permission is entirely uncertain. There is no fixed duration and no periodic term expressly agreed. However, the weekly payments may give rise to an implied periodic tenancy by the court treating the arrangement as a weekly periodic tenancy (by reference to the interval of payment), as occurred in Prudential Assurance v London Residuary Body where an uncertain term was held to give rise to a yearly periodic tenancy. Constance could terminate the implied periodic tenancy by appropriate notice. Sylvester is not a fixed-term tenant and cannot rely on the label as a protection. A: Incorrect — Street v Mountford overruled the label approach; courts look at substance. E: Incorrect — exclusive possession alone is necessary but not sufficient; certainty of term is also required. B: Incorrect — a periodic tenancy may be implied, but this does not transform the express arrangement into a fixed-term lease with the duration of the planning process; the parties have not agreed a periodic term expressly. C: Incorrect — while commercial leases receive more deference (Esso v Fumegrange), the threshold test (certainty of term and exclusive possession) still applies; the issue here is certainty, not deference to the label.

questions.scenario: Parveen and her partner Nico signed two separate written agreements with their landlord, Dominic, on the same day, each described as a 'licence to occupy' one room in Dominic's house. Each agreement stated that Dominic reserved the right to require Parveen and Nico to share the room with another occupier introduced by Dominic, and that they did not have exclusive use of any part of the property. In practice, Parveen and Nico shared the entire house together. Dominic never introduced any third party and had no intention of doing so. Parveen and Nico now claim they have a joint tenancy.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Parveen and Nico have a joint tenancy or individual licences?

options.A: Parveen and Nico are individual licensees because they signed separate agreements; separate agreements defeat the unity of title required for a joint tenancy.
options.B: Parveen and Nico are licensees because their agreements were labelled licences and they must be held to what they signed.
options.C: Parveen and Nico have individual tenancies of their respective rooms rather than a joint tenancy because exclusive possession of separate rooms gives rise to separate leases.
options.D: Parveen and Nico may have a joint tenancy — the court will look at the substance rather than the label; if they have exclusive possession of the whole property together, Street v Mountford applies and a tenancy may be found.
options.E: Parveen and Nico are licensees because the landlord retained a key and rights of access.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Street v Mountford requires courts to look at the substance of an arrangement rather than its label. The sharing and introduction clauses appear to be sham provisions inserted to avoid tenancy status (Antoniades v Villiers), particularly since Dominic had no genuine intention to introduce other occupiers and never did so. On unity of title: separate agreements do not necessarily defeat unity of title — if they are identical and interdependent (i.e. neither would have been signed without the other), unity of title is established (AG Securities v Vaughan; Antoniades v Villiers). Here, Parveen and Nico signed on the same day for the same property at the same time and their agreements appear interdependent. They had exclusive possession of the whole house together — together they could exclude all others, including Dominic. All four unities (possession, interest, time, title) are likely satisfied, giving rise to a joint tenancy. A: Incorrect — separate agreements do not automatically defeat unity of title where they are identical and interdependent. B: Incorrect — there is no mention of key retention on these facts; this is a distractor. C: Incorrect — Parveen and Nico did not have separate exclusive rooms; they shared the whole house, and there is no basis for individual tenancies of parts. E: Incorrect — Street v Mountford expressly rejected the label approach.

questions.scenario: Georgina is the freehold owner of a registered commercial unit. She verbally agrees to grant Haruto a ten-year lease of the unit at a market rent of £30,000 per year. Haruto moves into possession and pays rent monthly. No written document is ever signed. Haruto claims he has a legal lease. Georgina claims the arrangement is merely a monthly periodic tenancy. A third party, Inventum Ltd, subsequently purchases the freehold from Georgina and claims not to be bound by any arrangement with Haruto.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on the nature of Haruto's interest and its enforceability against Inventum Ltd?

options.A: Haruto has an equitable lease for ten years because equity treats the verbal agreement as a contract for lease; it is binding on Inventum as an overriding interest.
options.B: Haruto has a legal ten-year lease because he is in possession and paying rent at a market rate, which satisfies s 54(2) LPA 1925.
options.C: Haruto has a legal ten-year lease because he has been in possession for over one year, which is equivalent to registration.
options.D: Haruto has no interest of any kind because a purely verbal agreement for a lease of any length is void under LPA 1925 s 52.
options.E: Haruto does not have a legal ten-year lease because a lease over seven years must be created by deed and registered (LRA 2002 s 27(2)(b))

questions.explanation: E is correct. A ten-year lease is over seven years and must be: created by deed (s 52 LPA 1925; s 1 LP(MP)A 1989); and registered (s 27(2)(b)(i) LRA 2002). There is no deed. Without a valid deed there can be no legal ten-year lease. An equitable lease could arise if there were an enforceable contract for a lease complying with s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 (in writing, all terms, signed by both parties). There is no such document here — the agreement was entirely verbal. So no equitable lease arises under Walsh v Lonsdale. However, Haruto's possession and monthly rent payments give rise to an implied monthly periodic tenancy — the term is determined by reference to the period by which rent is calculated. A monthly periodic tenancy for one period of one month satisfies the s 54(2) LPA 1925 exception (three years or less, at market rent, taking effect in possession, no premium). It is therefore a legal parol lease, and as a lease of seven years or less it is an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 1 LRA 2002, binding on Inventum. B: Incorrect — s 54(2) only applies to leases of three years or less; a ten-year lease cannot be a parol lease. A: Incorrect — an equitable lease requires a written agreement satisfying s 2 LP(MP)A 1989; a verbal agreement is insufficient. C: Incorrect — possession alone does not substitute for registration. D: Incorrect — a monthly periodic tenancy can arise impliedly from conduct without formalities under s 54(2).

questions.tag: Service Occupancy and Intention to Create Legal Relations
questions.scenario: Redwood Academy is a boarding school. It provides accommodation to its caretaker, Marvin, in a cottage on the school grounds. The agreement states that Marvin is required to reside in the cottage 'for the better performance of his duties'. His employment contract requires him to be on site at all times outside school hours for security purposes. Marvin pays a reduced rent of £200 per month, well below market rate. Marvin argues he is a tenant with security of tenure. The Academy argues he is a service occupier.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Marvin has a lease or a service occupancy?

options.A: Marvin has a tenancy because the agreement was in writing and signed, satisfying s 2 LP(MP)A 1989.
options.B: Marvin is a licensee only because his agreement is described as a 'licence' and he is an employee of the Academy.
options.C: Marvin has a tenancy because he pays rent, and rent is the defining characteristic of a lease.
options.D: Marvin is a service occupier rather than a tenant because his residence is required to perform his duties, making the occupation incidental to his employment contract.
options.E: Marvin has a tenancy because the Academy charged rent, demonstrating an intention to create a commercial landlord-tenant relationship rather than a service occupancy.

questions.explanation: D is correct. A service occupancy arises where the occupier is required to reside in the premises for the better performance of their duties as an employee (Norris v Checksfield). The key distinction from a mere perk is whether occupation is essential to enable the employee to carry out their role — not merely convenient or advantageous. Here, Marvin's employment contract expressly requires him to be on-site at all times outside school hours for security purposes. The cottage on school grounds is the only means by which he can fulfil this contractual requirement. This is directly analogous to Norris v Checksfield (lorry driver required to live on premises) and distinguishable from Royal Philanthropic v County (teacher whose home was near the school but not necessary for his duties). The fact that Marvin pays rent does not convert the arrangement into a tenancy where the overriding characteristic is the contractual necessity of residing there for work performance. A: Incorrect — rent alone is not determinative; LPA 1925 s 205(1)(xxvii) confirms rent is not essential for a lease, and payment of rent does not automatically create a lease (it is also payable by lodgers and in other arrangements). C: Incorrect — s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 governs contracts for the disposition of a land interest; it is not the test for whether an occupier has a lease or service occupancy. E: Incorrect — the intention to charge rent is consistent with a service occupancy as well as a tenancy; it is not determinative. B: Incorrect — Street v Mountford rejects the label approach; the substance of the arrangement governs.

questions.scenario: Graeme owns a registered freehold property in his sole name. His wife Heloise has no legal interest in the property but has lived there for 12 years. Without Heloise's knowledge, Graeme approaches Meridian Bank and requests a mortgage of £400,000. Meridian's branch manager meets Graeme alone and accepts his assurances that he is the sole adult occupier. The mortgage is executed by deed and registered. Graeme defaults. Meridian seeks possession. Heloise claims that she was not properly advised of the risks of the mortgage, that Graeme obtained her subsequent signature to a consent form by threatening her with eviction, and that she has a beneficial interest under a constructive trust.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Heloise on whether Meridian's mortgage is binding on her?

options.A: Heloise can set aside the entire mortgage against Meridian because she was not party to it; the mortgage is void for want of her consent as a cohabiting spouse.
options.B: Heloise's only remedy is to bring a claim for damages against Graeme; the mortgage binds her because she signed a consent form.
options.C: Meridian's mortgage is fully binding on Heloise because only the legal owner can be a mortgagor, and Graeme was the sole legal owner; Heloise has no locus standi.
options.D: Heloise may challenge the mortgage on two grounds: an overriding interest if she was in actual occupation with a prior beneficial interest; and unenforceability if Meridian had notice of Graeme's undue influence.
options.E: Meridian cannot enforce the mortgage because it failed to independently advise Heloise before the mortgage was granted.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Heloise may have two independent but related grounds. First, on her beneficial interest: if Heloise has a constructive trust interest (from contribution or common intention) and was in actual occupation when the mortgage was registered, she may have an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002. Meridian's failure to inspect and its reliance on Graeme's unverified assurance that he is the sole occupier are significant — Meridian cannot rely on the 'not obvious on inspection' exception if it chose not to inspect (Abbey National BS v Cann). Second, on undue influence: where a lender takes a guarantee or security interest from a third party (Heloise) in a domestic relationship, it is put on inquiry (Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2)). The lender must take reasonable steps to ensure the surety received independent legal advice. If Meridian failed to do this and Graeme did exert undue influence, Meridian cannot rely on Heloise's tainted consent. C: Incorrect — a cohabiting partner with a constructive trust interest can have rights that affect the enforceability of a mortgage. A: Incorrect — if the mortgage was obtained by undue influence and Meridian had notice, it can be set aside against Meridian; consent form signatures obtained by undue influence are voidable. B: Incorrect — Heloise cannot void the entire legal mortgage; her remedy, if established, is that the mortgage is unenforceable against her interest, not that the mortgage itself is void. E: Incorrect — Meridian does not have a duty to personally advise the surety; it must take reasonable steps to ensure independent advice was received (Etridge).

questions.tag: Registered Land — Mirror Principle and Curtain Principle
questions.scenario: Tabitha purchases a registered freehold property from Ulrich for £800,000. Her solicitors carry out official searches of the register, which reveal no encumbrances on the title. Unknown to Tabitha, Ulrich had verbally promised his cousin Vincent that Vincent could use a shed on the property for storage, and Vincent has been using the shed once a week for two years. Ulrich also owed money to a construction company, and a charging order (a form of equitable charge) had been placed over the property but by clerical error at the Land Registry was never noted on the register. Tabitha completes the purchase. Vincent and the construction company both claim priority over Tabitha.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes Tabitha's position in relation to Vincent and the construction company?

options.A: Tabitha is bound by Vincent's storage right as an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 because he was in actual occupation of the shed; she is not bound by the charging order.
options.B: Both interests are binding on Tabitha: the charging order is a registered disposition and the verbal storage right is a constructive trust arising from Ulrich's promise.
options.C: Vincent's storage use is a licence — a personal right not enforceable against Tabitha. The charging order, as an unprotected equitable interest, is also not binding on Tabitha as a purchaser for value under s 29 LRA 2002.
options.D: Tabitha is bound by both Vincent's right and the charging order because she is a successor in title and takes land subject to all existing rights.
options.E: Tabitha is bound by the charging order because the construction company had a valid equitable charge; she is not bound by Vincent's licence.

questions.explanation: C is correct. On Vincent: a verbal permission to use a shed is a licence — a personal right only. It is not a proprietary right and cannot be enforced against a third party purchaser such as Tabitha, regardless of Vincent's physical use of the shed. Licences are not within the categories of overriding interests under Sch 3. On the charging order: this is an equitable interest in the land. Under s 29 LRA 2002, an equitable interest that is not protected by entry on the register and is not an overriding interest is not binding on a purchaser for valuable consideration (s 29(1)). Charging orders are not within Sch 3 para 1 (leases), Sch 3 para 2 (actual occupation — there is no occupier advancing this claim), or Sch 3 para 3 (easements). The construction company had the opportunity to protect its interest by notice; failure to do so means Tabitha takes free. D: Incorrect — successors in title do not take subject to all existing rights; s 29 LRA 2002 significantly qualifies this. E: Incorrect — an unprotected equitable charge is not binding on a purchaser for value under LRA 2002. A: Incorrect — Vincent is a licensee, not someone in actual occupation with a proprietary interest capable of overriding; Sch 3 para 2 requires an interest in the land, which a licence is not. B: Incorrect — a verbal assurance with no detrimental reliance does not automatically create a constructive trust; charging orders are equitable interests, not registered dispositions.

questions.scenario: Pinehurst Developments Ltd sells plots on a residential estate. Each buyer enters into a covenant with Pinehurst not to use their plot for any purpose other than as a private residence. The transfer deeds state that the covenants are imposed 'for the benefit of the estate'. Two plots are later acquired by commercial developers: one by Maxfield Hotels Ltd, which plans a boutique hotel, and one by a buyer who sells on to Radoslav. Radoslav's seller did not mention the covenant in the transfer. Maxfield now builds the hotel and Radoslav threatens to open a café. Other residents seek to enforce the covenants against both.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on the enforceability of the residential use covenant against Maxfield and Radoslav?

options.A: The covenant is enforceable against Maxfield only by Pinehurst Developments Ltd as the original covenantee, not by individual plot owners.
options.B: Neither Maxfield nor Radoslav is bound by the covenant because it was imposed by a developer and not between neighbouring landowners.
options.C: Only Maxfield is bound because its title shows the covenant; Radoslav is not bound because the covenant was not mentioned in his transfer.
options.D: The covenant is not enforceable against either because it is positive in nature — requiring occupiers to use property as a residence imposes an obligation.
options.E: Both Maxfield and Radoslav may be bound by the covenant if a building scheme (Elliston v Reacher) exists — all purchasers within the scheme are bound by and can enforce the mutually imposed obligations.

questions.explanation: E is correct. A building scheme (Elliston v Reacher conditions) allows the burden and benefit of restrictive covenants to be mutually enforceable between all plot owners on an estate where: (1) a common vendor sells plots laid out in a defined area; (2) the vendor imposed a common scheme of covenants for the benefit of all plots; (3) each buyer purchased on the basis that the covenants were for the mutual benefit of all plots; and (4) all buyers had notice of the scheme. If these conditions are met, each plot owner has the benefit of covenants given by all others, and each is bound by the covenants whether or not the covenant was mentioned in their individual transfer — the scheme is the notice. Radoslav takes bound because the building scheme gives every plot owner notice. Maxfield is bound as a successor covenantor if the burden passes (Tulk v Moxhay) and the covenant is registered. The covenant not to use for anything other than a private residence is restrictive. B: Incorrect — a building scheme operates between successors in title of the developer's plots, not only as between the developer and direct buyers. C: Incorrect — a building scheme binds all owners regardless of whether their individual transfer recited the covenant. D: Incorrect — a covenant not to use for purposes other than a private residence is restrictive in substance (it constrains what the owner can do); the 'hand in pocket' test is not engaged. A: Incorrect — building scheme covenants are mutually enforceable by and against all plot owners, not only by the original covenantee.

questions.scenario: Lavinia holds the benefit of a registered express legal easement of way over a path crossing Mervyn's registered freehold land. The path has been unused for eight years because Lavinia found a more convenient alternative route to her dominant land. Mervyn erects a fence over the path. Lavinia now wishes to use the original path because the alternative route has become impassable. Mervyn argues the easement has been extinguished by abandonment.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Lavinia and Mervyn on whether the easement has been extinguished?

options.A: Lavinia can only enforce the easement if she can demonstrate she used it within the last twelve months; eight years of non-use defeats all claims.
options.B: The easement is extinguished because the dominant and servient land are now both in the same ownership, satisfying the merger rule.
options.C: The easement has been extinguished because Lavinia has not used it for eight years; easements are extinguished automatically after seven years of non-use.
options.D: The easement has not necessarily been extinguished because abandonment requires clear and unequivocal acts of permanent intention; non-use alone is insufficient under Benn v Hardinge.
options.E: The easement has been extinguished because Mervyn has erected a fence, which constitutes a unilateral act of extinguishment by the servient owner.

questions.explanation: D is correct. An easement is not easily extinguished. The main methods of extinguishment include merger (common ownership), release (by deed or implication), and abandonment. Abandonment requires the dominant owner to demonstrate, by unambiguous and unequivocal acts, an intention to permanently abandon the right — not merely to have stopped using it for a period (Benn v Hardinge). Courts are slow to find abandonment. Eight years of non-use, even combined with use of an alternative route, does not automatically establish permanent abandonment — many easements are intermittently used, and the dominant owner's subjective or demonstrated intention matters. The fact that the alternative route has now become impassable reinforces that Lavinia never intended to permanently abandon the right. Moreover, the easement is registered; it appears on the register and would require formal removal to be extinguished. Mervyn cannot unilaterally extinguish an easement by erecting a fence — this is interference, not extinguishment. C: Incorrect — there is no seven-year (or any fixed-period) rule for automatic extinguishment of easements by non-use. E: Incorrect — erection of a fence is a trespass against the easement, not a mechanism of extinguishment. B: Incorrect — there is no suggestion of common ownership; the dominant and servient land remain separately owned. A: Incorrect — there is no one-year rule for enforcement; the Sch 3 para 3 one-year rule applies to whether an implied legal easement overrides a registered disposition, not to enforcement of a registered easement.

questions.scenario: Rania and Stefan purchase a flat registered in both their names for £250,000. Rania contributes £100,000 from her savings and Stefan contributes £50,000. The remaining £100,000 is funded by a joint mortgage in both names. The transfer deed contains an express declaration of trust stating they hold as 'joint tenants'. Two years later, their relationship ends and they dispute their shares. Rania argues she is entitled to a greater share based on her larger contribution. Stefan relies on the express declaration of trust.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on the beneficial shares of Rania and Stefan?

options.A: Rania is entitled to a 60% share and Stefan to 40%, reflecting their relative initial contributions to the purchase price (£150,000 effective contribution vs £100,000 counting equal mortgage liability).
options.B: The express declaration of trust as 'joint tenants' is conclusive between the parties and overrides arguments based on unequal contributions.
options.C: The express declaration is irrelevant because Rania paid the larger deposit; courts always follow contributions over declarations of trust.
options.D: Rania is entitled to the greater share because the court will apply the resulting trust presumption based on relative financial contributions to the purchase price.
options.E: Rania and Stefan each hold 50% as tenants in common, because 'joint tenants' in a trust context means equal but separate shares.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Where there is a valid express declaration of trust, it is conclusive of the beneficial shares between the parties (Goodman v Gallant). The declaration that they hold as 'joint tenants' means both the legal and equitable titles are held as joint tenants — they are treated as a single entity with the right of survivorship, not as having separate shares. An express declaration overrides any presumption that might otherwise arise from financial contributions. The only exceptions are fraud or mistake — there is no indication of either here. A: Incorrect — financial contributions to the purchase do not override a valid express declaration. C: Incorrect — the resulting trust presumption from contributions is displaced by an express declaration of trust. D: Incorrect — courts consistently hold that an express declaration of trust is binding and overrides contribution-based arguments (Goodman v Gallant). E: Incorrect — 'joint tenants' means they hold as one collective entity, not as tenants in common with separate shares; joint tenancy involves the four unities and right of survivorship.

questions.tag: Equitable Leases and Walsh v Lonsdale
questions.scenario: Catriona enters into a written agreement with her landlord, Declan, for a seven-year lease of a commercial unit at an annual rent of £24,000, to be paid quarterly in advance. The agreement is signed by both parties and contains all agreed terms, but no deed is executed and the lease is not registered. Declan demands the first quarterly payment and Catriona pays. Declan then purports to terminate the arrangement on the basis that no legal lease exists. He also argues that, under the common law, rent is payable annually in arrear, not quarterly in advance as the written agreement stipulates.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Catriona on her interest and the rent payment issue?

options.A: The equitable lease entitles Catriona to remain in possession but rent is payable under the common law rule (annually in arrear) because equitable leases cannot override common law rent rules.
options.B: Catriona has no interest because the lease was not completed by registration as required by LRA 2002 s 27 for a seven-year lease; failure to register means the lease does not exist.
options.C: Catriona has an equitable lease: the written agreement satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 and equity treats it as a contract to create a legal lease under Walsh v Lonsdale.
options.D: Catriona has only a monthly periodic tenancy implied from her possession and rent payment; the written agreement creates no enforceable lease.
options.E: Catriona has a legal seven-year lease because she is in possession and has paid rent, which satisfies s 54(2) LPA 1925.

questions.explanation: C is correct. A seven-year lease must be created by deed (s 52 LPA 1925) and registered (s 27(2)(b) LRA 2002) to be a legal lease. No deed was executed, so there is no legal lease. However, the written agreement satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 (in writing, all agreed terms, signed by both parties). Equity will treat this as a contract to create a legal lease enforceable by specific performance, creating an equitable lease on the terms of the agreement (Walsh v Lonsdale). Under Walsh v Lonsdale, where there is a conflict between the common law position and the terms of the equitable lease, equity prevails — the equitable lease is treated as if the legal lease existed in equity, meaning the terms of the agreement (quarterly in advance) apply. Declan cannot revert to the common law annual-in-arrear rule. Declan cannot unilaterally terminate because specific performance is available to Catriona. A: Incorrect — s 54(2) applies to leases of three years or less; it does not apply to a seven-year lease. D: Incorrect — the written agreement satisfying s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 creates an equitable lease, not merely an implied periodic tenancy; the implied periodic tenancy would only arise where no such agreement exists. B: Incorrect — failure to register means no legal lease exists, but an equitable lease can still arise from a valid contract under Walsh v Lonsdale. E: Incorrect — Walsh v Lonsdale expressly holds that where there is an equitable lease, equity prevails and the contractual terms govern.

questions.scenario: In January 2024, Fenella is granted an equitable easement of drainage over Gideon's registered freehold land. Fenella's solicitors protect it by a notice on the charges register of Gideon's title in March 2024. In May 2024, Gideon grants a legal mortgage to Pelican Finance Ltd, which is registered. In June 2024, Gideon sells the freehold to Holly for full market value; the transfer is registered. Holly claims not to be bound by Fenella's easement. Pelican also argues its mortgage should take priority over the easement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on the priority of Fenella's easement as against Pelican and Holly?

options.A: Fenella's easement is binding on both Pelican and Holly because it was registered before both the mortgage and the transfer.
options.B: Pelican's legal mortgage, registered in May 2024, takes priority over Fenella's equitable easement because legal interests always override equitable interests.
options.C: Fenella's easement is not binding on Holly because an equitable easement is not a registrable disposition and cannot bind a purchaser for value.
options.D: Fenella's equitable easement, protected by notice in March 2024, is binding on Holly as a purchaser for value under s 29(2) LRA 2002
options.E: Holly takes the property free of Fenella's easement because the transfer to Holly is the most recent registered disposition and therefore takes priority over all earlier interests.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under s 29(2) LRA 2002, a registrable disposition of a registered estate for valuable consideration takes free of prior interests except those protected on the register or overriding interests. Fenella protected her equitable easement by notice on the charges register in March 2024. This protection predates both Pelican's mortgage (May 2024) and Holly's purchase (June 2024). Under s 29(2), Holly as a purchaser for value is bound by interests protected on the register — Fenella's notice means Holly takes subject to the easement. On Pelican: Pelican's legal mortgage is a registrable disposition, and it too must take subject to prior interests protected on the register. Since Fenella's notice was entered in March 2024 before Pelican's mortgage in May 2024, Pelican takes subject to Fenella's easement. The earlier notice protects Fenella against both subsequent dispositions. A: Reaches the right conclusion but for potentially incomplete reasoning — the key mechanism is s 29(2) and the effect of the notice. B: Incorrect — in registered land, the LRA 2002 framework governs priority, not the old rule that legal interests prevail over equitable ones; a protected equitable interest binds later purchasers and mortgagees. C: Incorrect — equitable easements can be protected by notice under s 32 LRA 2002 and bind subsequent purchasers for value where so protected (s 29(2)). E: Incorrect — the chronological order of registration does not automatically give the most recent disposee priority over earlier protected interests.

questions.scenario: Alistair holds a leasehold interest of 10 years in a flat. He sublets three rooms to Bridget for a term of 15 years, purportedly for the full duration of the leasehold and beyond. He also grants his friend Charles a right to park on the communal car park attached to the building 'until the building is demolished'. Separately, Alistair's bank holds a legal mortgage over his leasehold interest, created by deed and registered.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best classifies the legal status of all three interests — Bridget's sublease, the parking right, and the bank's mortgage?

options.A: All three are equitable interests because they derive from a leasehold estate rather than the freehold.
options.B: Bridget has a lease for 10 years (the same term as Alistair's); the parking right is a legal easement because it has a definite start date; the bank's mortgage is equitable because it is over a leasehold, not a freehold.
options.C: Bridget has a freehold interest because her lease exceeds Alistair's leasehold and the excess converts into a freehold by operation of law.
options.D: Bridget has a legal leasehold interest for 15 years; the parking right is an equitable easement; the bank has a legal mortgage.
options.E: Bridget's sublease of 15 years cannot exceed Alistair's own 10-year leasehold — the rule that a sublease cannot exceed the head lease means the sublease is void for the excess term, leaving at most a 10-year sublease.

questions.explanation: E is correct. On Bridget's sublease: a sublease cannot be granted for a term equal to or longer than the superior lease; if it is, it operates as an assignment rather than a sublease. Alistair, with a 10-year lease, cannot sublet for 15 years. In practice, a purported sublease for a term at least as long as the head lease may be treated as an assignment or may operate only for a period less than the head lease. On the parking right: under s 1(2)(a) LPA 1925, a legal easement must be granted for a duration equivalent to a fee simple absolute in possession (forever) or a term of years absolute (a set period). 'Until the building is demolished' is for an uncertain period — it does not satisfy s 1(2)(a). It is therefore inherently equitable only (s 1(3) LPA 1925), provided it meets the minimum formality requirements for an equitable easement (s 53(1)(a) LPA 1925 — in writing and signed). On the bank's mortgage: LPA 1925 s 1(2)(c) includes mortgages as capable of being legal interests; a mortgage over a leasehold interest, created by deed and registered under s 27(2)(f) LRA 2002, is a valid legal mortgage. C: Incorrect on Bridget's sublease — a 15-year sublease from a 10-year head lease is not straightforwardly a valid legal 15-year lease. B: Multiple errors — the parking right fails certainty of term for a legal easement; mortgages over leaseholds can be legal. A: Incorrect — the nature of the superior estate does not prevent legal mortgages from being created over leasehold estates. D: Incorrect — this is not a recognised legal principle; an over-long sublease does not convert into a freehold.

questions.tag: Remedies — Appointment of Receiver and Foreclosure
questions.scenario: Whitmore Finance Ltd holds a registered legal mortgage over Jasper's commercial property. Jasper has defaulted on payments and Whitmore wishes to enforce its security. Jasper continues to operate his business from the property. Whitmore considers three options: (1) appointing a receiver; (2) taking possession itself and managing the property; (3) seeking foreclosure. Jasper's property is worth £1.2 million and the outstanding loan is £400,000.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Whitmore on the nature and practical implications of each remedy?

options.A: Whitmore can appoint a receiver under s 101(1)(iii) LPA 1925, allowing the debt to be serviced from income without Whitmore taking possession or incurring mortgagee-in-possession duties; foreclosure is a last resort the courts rarely grant.
options.B: Whitmore should seek foreclosure because it would give it outright ownership of a £1.2 million property in exchange for cancelling a £400,000 debt, and courts always grant foreclosure to mortgagees.
options.C: Whitmore can take possession immediately and run the business; it has no obligations to Jasper once it is in possession because Jasper is in default.
options.D: Foreclosure is the most appropriate remedy because it is faster than sale and avoids the Cuckmere Brick duty to obtain market value.
options.E: Whitmore must obtain a court order before appointing a receiver or taking possession, regardless of the circumstances.

questions.explanation: A is correct. On appointment of receiver: under LPA 1925 s 101(1)(iii) (and s 109), a legal mortgagee can appoint a receiver once the power of sale has arisen. The receiver acts as the agent of the mortgagor (Jasper) rather than Whitmore, which protects Whitmore from liability for the running of the business. This is a less intrusive remedy that keeps Whitmore at arm's length. On possession: a mortgagee in possession owes strict duties in equity — it must account for all income actually received and all that could have been received with due diligence (White v City of London Brewery). This exposes Whitmore to significant liability if it mismanages the business. On foreclosure: foreclosure is an equitable remedy that extinguishes the borrower's equity of redemption and vests the property in the mortgagee absolutely. Courts are very reluctant to grant it, particularly where there is a large surplus (here £800,000 over the debt) — courts usually order sale instead to ensure Jasper receives the surplus. Foreclosure produces a windfall inconsistent with the principle that mortgages are security for a loan, not an opportunity for enrichment. B: Incorrect — courts are highly unlikely to grant foreclosure where it would generate a large windfall to the lender; this is precisely the situation courts redirect to a sale order. E: Incorrect — appointment of a receiver does not require a court order; it is a contractual right of the mortgagee. D: Incorrect — foreclosure does not avoid the duty to account properly and it is far from fast; the court process is lengthy and courts rarely grant it. C: Incorrect — a mortgagee in possession has onerous equitable duties of management.

questions.tag: Common Law Rules — Benefit at Common Law
questions.scenario: In 1990, Wilbur covenanted with his neighbour Xanthe to maintain a party wall at his own expense. Xanthe sold her dominant land to Yasmin in 2005, expressly assigning the benefit of the covenant to Yasmin in writing with notice given to Wilbur. Wilbur sold his burdened land to Zara in 2010, also including an indemnity covenant requiring Zara to comply with the original covenant. The party wall is now in serious disrepair. Yasmin seeks to enforce the covenant.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Yasmin can enforce the party wall maintenance covenant against Zara?

options.A: Yasmin can enforce the covenant against both Wilbur and Zara because the express assignment to Yasmin and the indemnity covenant together create direct contractual privity between Yasmin and Zara.
options.B: Yasmin can enforce the covenant against Zara in equity because the covenant touches and concerns the land and the burden has passed under Tulk v Moxhay.
options.C: Yasmin can enforce the covenant against Wilbur in damages because the burden of a positive covenant does not run at common law or in equity; she has no direct claim against Zara unless an exception such as mutual benefit and burden applies.
options.D: Yasmin cannot enforce the covenant against Wilbur because the original covenantor's liability ends when he sells the burdened land.
options.E: Yasmin can enforce the covenant against Zara at common law because the benefit has been expressly assigned to Yasmin and the burden passes at common law since the covenant touches and concerns the land.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The party wall maintenance covenant is positive — Wilbur must spend money to maintain the wall. The burden of positive covenants does not pass to successors in equity (Rhone v Stephens; Haywood v Brunswick) or at common law (Austerberry v Oldham). Zara, as a successor to the burdened land, is not directly bound by the covenant. Yasmin cannot directly enforce the positive covenant against Zara. However, Wilbur remains liable under continuing liability of the original covenantor — this is either express in the covenant or implied by s 79(1) LPA 1925. Wilbur can be sued in damages for breaches committed by Zara. Wilbur's only recourse is the indemnity covenant chain: Wilbur can claim against Zara under the indemnity covenant he extracted on sale. The chain has only one link here (Wilbur → Zara), which may still be effective. Yasmin's only direct remedy against Wilbur is damages, not specific performance (since Wilbur no longer owns the land). E: Incorrect — the burden of positive covenants does not pass to successors at common law, despite what touches and concerns tests suggest about the benefit. B: Incorrect — Tulk v Moxhay operates only for restrictive covenants; it cannot be used to pass the burden of positive covenants. A: Incorrect — the indemnity covenant creates contractual rights between Wilbur and Zara only; Yasmin is not party to it. D: Incorrect — continuing liability of the original covenantor (Wilbur) persists even after sale of the burdened land.

questions.scenario: Two brothers, Alec and Bernard, purchased a house as beneficial joint tenants. Over the years they began to disagree about its future. Bernard's solicitor sends a letter to Alec stating: 'Our client wishes to discuss with you the possibility of severing the joint tenancy.' Three months later, Bernard makes a will leaving his share to his daughter. Six months after that, Alec and Bernard begin negotiating the sale of the property as if they each owned a 50% share. Bernard dies before any agreement is reached. Bernard's daughter claims the joint tenancy was severed.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether the joint tenancy was severed before Bernard's death?

options.A: The joint tenancy was severed by Bernard's will, which takes effect on his death and converts his interest into a tenancy in common.
options.B: The joint tenancy was not severed because a notice must show an immediate and irrevocable intention to sever; a letter seeking to discuss the possibility is insufficient, and a will cannot sever a joint tenancy.
options.C: The joint tenancy was severed by the solicitor's letter because any indication of an intention to sever in writing is sufficient.
options.D: The joint tenancy cannot be severed at all because Alec and Bernard hold as beneficial joint tenants and only a court order can convert this to a tenancy in common.
options.E: The joint tenancy was severed by Bernard's will and Bernard's daughter is entitled to a 50% share.

questions.explanation: B is correct. On the solicitor's letter: a notice of severance under s 196 LPA 1925 must demonstrate an immediate, unilateral and irrevocable intention to treat the joint tenancy as severed (Burgess v Rawnsley). A letter expressing a wish to 'discuss the possibility' of severance does not demonstrate an immediate intention to sever — it is merely a proposal to negotiate. It does not qualify as a valid notice. On the will: a will has no effect until after death. By the time a will takes effect, survivorship has already operated, vesting the deceased's interest in the survivor. A will cannot sever a joint tenancy (Re Caines). On mutual conduct: under Williams v Hensman, a joint tenancy can be severed by a course of dealing sufficient to intimate that the interests of all parties were mutually treated as constituting a tenancy in common. Negotiating a sale as if each owned 50% shares (Davis v Smith) may amount to severance by mutual conduct. If it does, the joint tenancy was severed before Bernard's death and his daughter inherits his 50% share under the will. A: Incorrect — a will cannot sever a joint tenancy; survivorship operates on death before the will takes effect. C: Incorrect — any written indication of a wish to discuss is insufficient; a notice must be unequivocal and immediate. E: Incorrect — same reason as A. D: Incorrect — severance can be effected unilaterally by written notice, by alienation, or by mutual conduct; no court order is required.

questions.tag: Multiple Occupancy — Joint Tenancy vs Individual Licences
questions.scenario: Odysseus and Penelope are friends who respond separately to an advertisement for rooms in a house owned by Telemon. They each visit the house on the same day and sign separate but identical written agreements on the same day for different amounts of monthly 'licence fees'. Their agreements state that they are each licensed to use a named room and share communal areas with others. In practice, they are the only occupants. Telemon occasionally sends cleaning staff once a week. Odysseus and Penelope argue they have a joint tenancy of the whole house.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Odysseus and Penelope have a joint tenancy?

options.A: Odysseus and Penelope have individual tenancies of their named rooms because each has a separate written agreement for a specific room.
options.B: Odysseus and Penelope have a joint tenancy because they signed identical agreements on the same day and together have exclusive occupation of the whole house.
options.C: Whether Odysseus and Penelope hold a joint tenancy depends on substance: the court will look through the licence label; a genuine cleaning service may defeat exclusive possession, but a sham arrangement will be disregarded.
options.D: Odysseus and Penelope are licensees because their agreements are described as licences; courts always follow the label in commercial contexts.
options.E: Odysseus and Penelope have a joint tenancy because they have exclusive use of the communal areas.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The analysis requires examining all four unities and the substance of the arrangement. On unity of title: separate agreements do not automatically defeat unity of title if they are identical and interdependent (Antoniades v Villiers). However, if the agreements differ (as here — different fees), this is a factor suggesting they are independent arrangements rather than a single joint tenancy. In AG Securities v Vaughan, agreements for different rooms at different times at different rents were held not to be a joint tenancy. On unity of interest: all joint tenants must have the same term, same conditions, and joint liability for the whole rent. If Odysseus pays one fee and Penelope another, and each is liable only for their own fee, unity of interest may be absent. On exclusive possession: the cleaning service, if genuinely provided (Marchant v Charters), defeats exclusive possession and creates a lodger relationship. If it is a sham inserted to avoid a tenancy, courts will disregard it. The outcome is therefore fact-sensitive and uncertain — hence the need to examine all these features. B: Incorrect — identical agreements alone are insufficient if the fees differ and unity of interest cannot be established. A: Incorrect — individual tenancies of separate rooms require each party to have exclusive possession of their specific area, but here they share communal areas and there is no clear demarcation. D: Incorrect — Street v Mountford rejected the label approach; courts look at substance. E: Incorrect — exclusive use of communal areas shared with each other does not establish the kind of exclusive possession (excluding all others including the landlord) required for a lease.

questions.tag: Land Charges — Effect of Non-Registration (Unregistered Land)
questions.scenario: In 1997, Veronique was granted an option to purchase an unregistered plot of land owned by her landlord, Wentworth. The option was not registered as a land charge. In 2005, Wentworth, aware of the option, sold the land to his son Xavier for £1 (nominal consideration). Xavier knew of the option. In 2010, Xavier sold the land to Yolanda, a buyer for full market value (£200,000) who did not know of the option. Veronique now seeks to exercise the option against Yolanda.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises Veronique on whether the option is enforceable against Xavier and Yolanda?

options.A: The option is void against both Xavier and Yolanda because it was not registered; non-registration makes it void against all parties including donees.
options.B: The option is binding on Xavier because he had actual knowledge of it; it is not binding on Yolanda because she purchased for value without notice.
options.C: The option is void against Yolanda as a purchaser for value under s 4(6) LCA 1972 because it was unregistered; once void it cannot be revived, and Xavier's position turns on whether nominal consideration makes him a purchaser.
options.D: The option is binding on Xavier because he had actual notice, and binding on Yolanda because she derives title through Xavier who was bound.
options.E: The option is void against Yolanda only if she registered the transfer before Veronique registered the option; since both registrations occurred after 1997, first in time wins.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 4(6) LCA 1972, an unregistered Class C(IV) land charge (estate contract, which includes options to purchase) is void against a purchaser of a legal estate for money or money's worth. On Xavier: the sale for £1 is nominally a sale for consideration. The courts have held (Midland Bank Trust Co Ltd v Green) that even nominal or grossly undervalue consideration can constitute 'money or money's worth' — the motive for the sale is irrelevant, and the court will not look behind the consideration. On the facts of Midland Bank Trust v Green, the son's unregistered option was void even against a sale at a gross undervalue (£500 for a £40,000 farm). If the same principle applies, Xavier as a technical purchaser for money could take free of the option. On Yolanda: even if Veronique's option survived to bind Xavier (which is uncertain), once an equitable interest is void against a purchaser for value, it is permanently destroyed and cannot revive against a subsequent purchaser — even one with notice. Yolanda therefore takes free. D: Incorrect — the doctrine of notice does not apply in the unregistered land charges context; entry on the register (or non-entry) is conclusive (s 198 LPA 1925; Midland Bank v Green). A: Incorrect — non-registration makes an interest void against purchasers for value of a legal estate, not against donees; a donee takes subject to the interest. B: Incorrect — actual notice is irrelevant in the land charges system. E: Incorrect — there is no 'first in time' registration race in this context; the result depends on whether the charge was registered before the relevant disposition.

questions.tag: Implied Acquisition — s 62 LPA 1925 Upgrade Effect
questions.scenario: Meadow Farm is owned by Loretta. She has historically allowed her tenant, Miles, to use a drainage channel crossing Meadow Farm's northern field, without any formal agreement, as a permissive informal arrangement. Miles vacates and Loretta re-lets the northern field to a new tenant, Nadia, by a formal legal lease for a term of five years. The lease makes no mention of the drainage channel. Nadia uses the drainage channel from day one. A year later, Loretta sells the freehold to Oscar, who blocks the drainage channel. Nadia claims an easement.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Nadia has an easement over the drainage channel under s 62 LPA 1925?

options.A: Nadia has no easement because the informal permissive use by Miles was interrupted when Miles vacated and a new tenancy commenced, breaking any continuity.
options.B: Nadia may have an easement under the upgrade effect of s 62 LPA 1925: the informal permissive use by Miles constitutes prior diversity of occupation (Miles as tenant, Loretta as landlord)
options.C: Nadia can only claim a prescriptive easement because 20 years of user is required; Miles' user cannot be aggregated with Nadia's for prescription purposes.
options.D: Nadia has no easement because s 62 LPA 1925 can only imply easements into transfer deeds, not leases.
options.E: Nadia has an easement under Wheeldon v Burrows because the drainage channel was in use by Loretta as a quasi-easement before the lease to Nadia.

questions.explanation: B is correct. The upgrade effect of s 62 LPA 1925 operates where: (1) there was prior diversity of occupation (Miles as tenant, Loretta as landlord); (2) an informal permission or licence was granted to the occupier of the dominant tenement to use the servient land (Loretta permitted Miles to use the drainage channel); and (3) there was a conveyance — here the legal lease granted to Nadia. When Nadia's legal lease was granted, s 62 operated to upgrade the previously permissive informal right into a full legal easement implied into the lease (Wright v Macadam). The implied easement is legal because it takes its status from the document it is implied into. As a legal implied easement over registered land, it may be an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 3 LRA 2002 if exercised within the last year — Nadia used it from day one and has exercised it throughout, so Oscar takes subject to it. D: Incorrect — s 62 applies to legal leases as well as transfer deeds; 'conveyance' under s 62 includes a legal lease. C: Incorrect — prescription requires 20 years of qualifying user; Miles' permissive use cannot found a prescriptive claim (permission defeats nec precario), and in any event Nadia has only used it for one year. E: Incorrect — Wheeldon v Burrows requires the quasi-easement to have been used by the common owner at the date of the grant; here the drainage was used by Miles as tenant, not by Loretta herself, so it was not a quasi-easement of Loretta's. A: Incorrect — the interruption in physical use when Miles vacated does not defeat s 62; what matters is that prior diversity of occupation existed and an informal permission had been granted during that time.

questions.tag: Registered Land — First Registration and Overriding Interests
questions.scenario: Conrad holds an unregistered freehold. He sells part of his land to Delia by transfer deed. Delia is required to register for the first time. Before she registers, the following interests exist over the land: (i) a legal mortgage granted by the previous owner and protected by deposit of title deeds; (ii) a visible path that Edith, a neighbouring landowner, has used openly without permission for 25 years; and (iii) an equitable restrictive covenant created in 1990, protected by a Class D(II) land charge registered against Conrad's name. Delia registers the land. She then argues she takes free of all three interests.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes which of the three interests bind Delia following first registration?

options.A: Only Edith's easement binds Delia; the mortgage and covenant are defeated by first registration.
options.B: The Class C(I) mortgage and Class D(II) covenant both bind Delia as registered charges; Edith's legal prescriptive easement is an overriding interest on first registration under Sch 1 para 3 LRA 2002.
options.C: The restrictive covenant does not bind Delia because Class D(II) land charges only bind subsequent purchasers of unregistered land, and once the land is registered all land charges are automatically vacated.
options.D: The mortgage binds Delia only if she had actual notice of it; the covenant and easement are both overriding interests that bind automatically.
options.E: Delia takes free of all three interests because registration gives her a clean title under the LRA 2002 mirror principle.

questions.explanation: B is correct. On the legal mortgage: a second legal mortgage (puisne mortgage) over unregistered land must be protected as a Class C(I) land charge; if properly registered, it binds all subsequent purchasers (s 198 LPA 1925) and on first registration it carries over as an overriding interest or noted charge binding Delia. On the restrictive covenant: a Class D(II) land charge, if validly registered against Conrad's name, is binding on all purchasers (s 198 LPA 1925); it continues to bind Delia. On Edith's easement: a prescriptive legal easement is a legal interest that binds the world over unregistered land; on first registration it is an overriding interest under Sch 1 para 3 LRA 2002. All three bind Delia. A: Incorrect — first registration does not wipe out pre-existing interests that are either registered as land charges or qualify as overriding interests on first registration. C: Incorrect — land charges registered before first registration continue to bind. D: Incorrect — actual notice is irrelevant in the land charges regime; registration is conclusive. E: Incorrect — the land charges system continues to operate until the land is registered; protected charges carry over and are noted on first registration.

questions.scenario: Isadora holds a registered freehold. She purports to grant a legal mortgage to Juniper Bank by signing a document described as a 'legal charge'. The document is in writing and contains all agreed terms, signed by Isadora and a representative of Juniper, but it was never witnessed and therefore does not comply with the deed requirements in LP(MP)A 1989 s 1. The document is submitted to the Land Registry but rejected. Isadora later defaults. Juniper seeks to enforce the mortgage as a legal charge. Isadora argues the mortgage is void.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on the status of Juniper's interest and its ability to enforce?

options.A: Juniper's purported legal mortgage failed for want of a valid deed; if the document satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989, equity will treat it as a contract to grant a legal mortgage and recognise an equitable charge.
options.B: Juniper has an equitable mortgage that is automatically an overriding interest under Sch 3 para 2 LRA 2002 because it was in actual occupation of the property.
options.C: Juniper has no enforceable interest because the document is not a valid deed and therefore the mortgage is void for all purposes.
options.D: Juniper has a legal mortgage because it was created in writing and signed by both parties, which satisfies the requirements of s 52 LPA 1925.
options.E: Juniper has a legal mortgage because the document was submitted to the Land Registry, and submission is equivalent to registration.

questions.explanation: A is correct. A legal mortgage over registered land must be created by a valid deed complying with LP(MP)A 1989 s 1 (in writing, clear on its face it is a deed, signed and witnessed) and registered under LRA 2002 s 27(2)(f). The document here was not witnessed and therefore is not a valid deed — no legal mortgage arises and, critically, the Land Registry rejected it so it was never registered. However, a defective legal mortgage may still be an equitable mortgage if it satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 — in writing, all agreed terms, signed by both parties. These conditions appear to be satisfied. Equity treats it as a contract to grant a legal mortgage. Juniper can enforce as an equitable mortgagee. The key practical limitation is that the LPA 1925 s 101 statutory power of sale (and the other remedies in Part III LPA 1925) apply only to a 'mortgagee where the mortgage is made by deed' — an equitable mortgagee cannot rely on these. Instead, Juniper must apply to court under TOLATA s 14. C: Incorrect — a defective legal mortgage can still be an equitable mortgage; it is not void for all purposes. D: Incorrect — s 52 LPA 1925 requires a deed; writing alone (without proper deed formalities including witnessing) is insufficient. E: Incorrect — submission to the Land Registry is not equivalent to registration; registration requires acceptance by the Registry. B: Incorrect — an equitable mortgagee is not in 'actual occupation' of the mortgaged property; Sch 3 para 2 protects persons with interests who are physically in occupation.

questions.tag: Schemes of Development and Benefit in Equity — Annexation vs Building Scheme
questions.scenario: Maplewood Developments Ltd sells plots on a new residential estate. Each buyer covenants not to use their property for business purposes. The conveyances state the covenants are 'for the benefit and protection of the other plots on the estate'. After several years, Norbert, who bought a plot, wishes to open a home office offering commercial services. His neighbour, Odette, who bought her plot from a later buyer of the estate, seeks to enforce the covenant against Norbert. Norbert argues Odette cannot enforce because the benefit was not expressly assigned to Odette's predecessor, and there is no building scheme.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether Odette can enforce the covenant against Norbert?

options.A: Odette cannot enforce the covenant because she must prove an unbroken chain of express assignments of the benefit from Maplewood to each successive owner.
options.B: Odette cannot enforce the covenant against Norbert because a covenant against commercial use is positive in nature and positive covenants do not run with the land.
options.C: Odette can enforce the covenant only if she can show that Maplewood Developments Ltd retained land at the time of creating the covenant and is still willing to enforce it.
options.D: Odette can enforce the covenant if the benefit was annexed under s 78 LPA 1925 (passing automatically to successors), or if it was expressly assigned to her; the burden has run in equity as a registered restrictive covenant.
options.E: Odette can enforce the covenant because the benefit passes automatically to all successors at common law without any need for annexation or assignment.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The benefit of a restrictive covenant can pass in equity by: (i) express or implied annexation; (ii) express assignment; or (iii) building scheme. On annexation: s 78 LPA 1925, as interpreted in Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties, provides that a covenant relating to land is made with successors in title and operates to annex the benefit automatically. The wording 'for the benefit and protection of the other plots on the estate' further supports express annexation, and s 78 reinforces this. If annexed, the benefit passes to Odette without express assignment. On building scheme: if the Elliston v Reacher conditions are satisfied, a scheme of mutually enforceable covenants exists, giving every plot owner the benefit of covenants by all others. The burden must also pass via Tulk v Moxhay (the covenant is restrictive; it must be protected on the register). B: Incorrect — express assignment is only one of three routes; annexation (especially under s 78 and Federated Homes) is sufficient. C: Incorrect — a covenant not to use property for business is restrictive in substance (it restricts what the owner does with the land); the 'hand in pocket' test is not engaged. E: Incorrect — at common law the benefit of covenants passes more restrictively; the common law rules are different from equitable annexation. A: Incorrect — the original covenantee (Maplewood) need not retain land or be willing to enforce; once the benefit passes to successors, they can enforce independently.

questions.tag: Four Unities — Joint Tenancy and Legal Title
questions.scenario: A freehold property is transferred to five people: Petra, Quinn, Rowan, Sasha, and Tomas. The transfer deed names them all. All five are adults of sound mind. The deed states they are to hold the property 'on trust for themselves as beneficial tenants in common in equal shares'. Quinn subsequently becomes bankrupt. His trustee in bankruptcy claims an interest in the property. Petra argues Quinn has no identifiable share because the legal title must be held as a joint tenancy.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the legal and beneficial ownership of the property?

options.A: The first four named sui juris individuals (Petra, Quinn, Rowan, and Sasha) hold legal title as trustees; Tobias and Uma hold only equitable interests; the maximum of four legal owners applies under s 34 LPA 1925.
options.B: All five hold the legal title; the right of survivorship applies to the equitable title since the deed uses the word 'trust'.
options.C: All five hold the legal title as joint tenants; the beneficial tenancy in common declared in the deed is void because it conflicts with the joint tenancy at law.
options.D: The transfer is void because more than four people are named and land cannot be held by more than four people in any capacity.
options.E: All five hold both the legal and equitable title as tenants in common in equal shares, as stated in the transfer deed.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under LPA 1925 s 34(2) and Trustee Act 1925 s 34, a maximum of four people can hold the legal title to land. Where more than four are named, the first four named who are sui juris (over 18 and of sound mind) become the legal title holders. All five named here are adults of sound mind, so the first four — Petra, Quinn, Rowan, and Sasha — hold the legal title as trustees. The fifth (Tomas) does not hold the legal title but holds a beneficial interest only. The legal title must be held as a joint tenancy (LPA 1925 s 1(6)). The equitable or beneficial title is separate — the deed expressly declares a beneficial tenancy in common in equal shares, which is valid and takes effect in equity. Each of the five has a 20% beneficial share as a tenant in common. Since tenants in common have distinct (albeit undivided) shares, Quinn's 20% beneficial share is an identifiable asset that vests in his trustee in bankruptcy on the bankruptcy order. D: Incorrect — the legal title cannot be held by more than four; only the first four can be legal owners. C: Incorrect — the legal title must be held as a joint tenancy, but this does not invalidate a separate equitable tenancy in common declared in the deed. E: Incorrect — the transfer is valid; the law limits the legal title holders to four but allows all five beneficial shares. B: Incorrect — survivorship applies to the joint tenancy at law, not to the equitable tenancy in common where each beneficiary has a separate identifiable share that passes under their will or intestacy.

questions.tag: Certainty of Term — Periodic Tenancies and Break Clauses
questions.scenario: Hamelin Properties Ltd grants a five-year fixed term lease of a retail unit to Ingrid. The lease contains a break clause allowing Hamelin to terminate the lease at any time on three months' written notice 'if the unit is required for Hamelin's own use'. The lease also states that rent is due on the first day of each quarter. In year three, Hamelin serves a notice purporting to exercise the break clause, stating it wishes to use the unit for its own offices. Ingrid argues the break clause is uncertain and void, and that the lease therefore continues for the full five-year term.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best advises on whether the break clause is valid and Hamelin can terminate the lease?

options.A: The break clause is void for uncertainty because 'required for Hamelin's own use' is a subjective condition that cannot be objectively assessed, so the lease runs for the full five years.
options.B: The break clause is void because a landlord can never unilaterally terminate a fixed-term lease before the end of the term; only a tenant can exercise a break clause.
options.C: The break clause is valid — a fixed-term lease can contain a break clause; conditionality ('if required for own use') does not make it uncertain, and the landlord may exercise it once the condition is satisfied.
options.D: The break clause is valid but Hamelin must obtain a court order before it takes effect because Ingrid is in occupation of the premises.
options.E: The break clause makes the lease a periodic tenancy rather than a fixed-term lease, because the possibility of early termination means the maximum duration is not known from the outset.

questions.explanation: C is correct. A fixed-term lease can validly include a break clause allowing one or both parties to bring the lease to an early end — this is a recognised and common feature of commercial leases. The inclusion of a break clause does not undermine the certainty of term required by Street v Mountford: the maximum duration remains known (five years), and the break clause merely provides an option to end the lease earlier. A conditional break clause ('if the unit is required for own use') is also valid — the condition can be assessed objectively by a court. Hamelin must serve the notice in the correct form (three months' written notice) and must genuinely require the unit for its own use. If these conditions are met, the break clause is validly exercised and the lease ends on the expiry of the notice period. A: Incorrect — conditional break clauses are routinely upheld in commercial leases; they are not void for uncertainty merely because they include a qualifying condition. B: Incorrect — break clauses can be drafted in favour of the landlord, the tenant, or both; there is no rule that only tenants can exercise break clauses. E: Incorrect — the presence of a break clause does not convert a fixed-term lease into a periodic tenancy; the maximum term is still known (five years). D: Incorrect — no court order is required to exercise a valid contractual break clause; it is a right under the lease itself.

questions.scenario: Kezia owns a registered freehold. In January 2025 she grants a legal mortgage to Larkspur Bank, which is registered. In March 2025 she grants a second equitable mortgage to Mallard Finance Ltd by a written agreement satisfying s 2 LP(MP)A 1989; Mallard protects this by a notice on the charges register. In June 2025 she grants a third legal mortgage to Nightingale Lenders Ltd, which is also registered. Kezia defaults on all three. The property is sold under the power of sale for £500,000. Larkspur is owed £300,000, Mallard is owed £150,000, and Nightingale is owed £200,000. The surplus is to be distributed.
questions.stem: Which one of the following best describes the order in which the three mortgagees are paid from the proceeds of sale?

options.A: Nightingale is paid first because it holds a legal mortgage; then Larkspur; then Mallard because equitable mortgages are always paid last.
options.B: Nightingale is paid last because it registered after Mallard entered its notice, and a later registered legal mortgage takes subject to a prior protected equitable interest.
options.C: Larkspur is paid first as first registered; Mallard second as a prior protected equitable mortgage; Nightingale last from the remainder and is an unsecured creditor for any shortfall.
options.D: All three mortgagees share the proceeds proportionately because they all have valid security interests in the property.
options.E: Mallard is paid last because equitable interests cannot take priority over a subsequent legal mortgage.

questions.explanation: C is correct. The priority rules under LRA 2002 apply. Larkspur registered first in January 2025 — it is the first registered legal mortgage and takes priority under s 29 LRA 2002 over all subsequent interests. Mallard entered a notice in March 2025 — this protected its equitable mortgage on the register before Nightingale's registration in June 2025. Under s 29(2) LRA 2002, a later registrable disposition (Nightingale's mortgage) takes subject to interests protected on the register at the time of its registration. Since Mallard's notice was on the register when Nightingale registered in June 2025, Nightingale takes subject to Mallard's equitable mortgage. The order of payment from the £500,000 proceeds is therefore: (1) Larkspur: £300,000; (2) Mallard: £150,000; (3) Nightingale: £50,000 (the remainder), with £150,000 of Nightingale's debt unsatisfied. E: Incorrect — priority in registered land follows the order of registration and protection on the register, not simply legal vs equitable status. D: Incorrect — mortgagees do not share proceeds proportionately; the first in priority is paid in full before later creditors receive anything. B: Incorrect — Mallard's notice was entered before Nightingale's registration, giving Mallard priority over Nightingale under s 29(2). A: Correct as to Nightingale's position but incomplete — B provides the full and accurate analysis of all three priorities.

questions.scenario: Harriet owns a house by the coast and allows her friend Marcus to stay in a spare bedroom for a small weekly payment. The arrangement is described as a licence. Harriet sells the house to Priya.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes Marcus's legal position against Priya?

options.A: Marcus cannot enforce his right to stay against Priya because a licence is a personal right that only binds the original parties.
options.B: Marcus can enforce his right to stay against Priya because he is in actual occupation of the property.
options.C: Marcus can enforce his right to stay against Priya because he has been paying a weekly sum, which creates a proprietary interest.
options.D: Marcus can enforce his right to stay against Priya because the arrangement satisfies the requirements of a lease.
options.E: Marcus cannot enforce his right against Priya, but only because Harriet failed to register the licence at the Land Registry.

questions.explanation: A is correct. A licence is a personal right, not a proprietary right. It only binds the original parties and cannot be enforced against a third party such as a new owner. Marcus's only recourse would be a personal action for damages against Harriet for breach of contract. D: Wrong because payment alone does not convert a licence into a proprietary interest; the nature of the right, not the consideration, determines its status. B: Wrong because actual occupation is relevant to the enforcement of proprietary interests (such as beneficial interests under a trust) as overriding interests, but a licence is not a proprietary interest and so actual occupation does not assist Marcus. E: Wrong because there is no indication that the arrangement satisfies the essential requirements of a lease (certainty of term and exclusive possession); a licence to stay in a spare bedroom is unlikely to amount to exclusive possession. C: Wrong because the reason a licence is unenforceable against a third party is that it is a personal right by nature, not because of any failure to register; licences cannot be registered at the Land Registry.

questions.scenario: Oliver purchases a country house from Beatrice. The contract is silent on which items are included in the sale. After completion, Oliver discovers that Beatrice has removed an ornate marble fireplace surround that was bolted to the chimney breast, and an antique grandfather clock that was freestanding on the floor of the hallway.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes Oliver's legal position?

options.A: Oliver can recover both the fireplace surround and the grandfather clock because all items inside a house at the point of sale automatically pass with the conveyance.
options.B: Oliver can recover the fireplace surround but not the grandfather clock, because the surround is likely a fixture while the clock is likely a chattel.
options.C: Oliver cannot recover either item because Beatrice removed them before completion and no items were expressly included in the contract.
options.D: Oliver can recover the grandfather clock but not the fireplace surround, because the clock has greater monetary value and therefore forms part of the land.
options.E: Oliver can recover both items because the Holland v Hodgson test treats any item resting on its own weight as a fixture.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under the Holland v Hodgson test, the degree and purpose of annexation determine whether an item is a fixture or a chattel. The marble fireplace surround, bolted to the chimney breast, is firmly affixed and its purpose is to enhance the building — it is likely a fixture forming part of the land. The grandfather clock, freestanding on the floor, rests by its own weight and its purpose is its enjoyment as a chattel — it is likely a chattel. Under s 62 LPA 1925, a conveyance automatically includes all fixtures unless specifically excluded. A: Wrong because not all items inside a house are fixtures; the Holland v Hodgson test must be applied to determine whether an item is a fixture or a chattel. C: Wrong because the timing of removal is irrelevant if the items are fixtures; if the contract is silent, s 62 LPA 1925 means fixtures pass with the conveyance and the seller may not remove them after contracting to sell. D: Wrong because monetary value is not the relevant test; the test is degree and purpose of annexation. E: Wrong because items resting on their own weight are prima facie chattels, not fixtures — the Holland v Hodgson test creates the opposite presumption to that stated.

questions.scenario: Tanya agrees to buy Farid's registered freehold house. Contracts are exchanged and a deposit is paid. A valid deed of transfer is executed by Farid and dated. However, before the deed is submitted to the Land Registry, Farid changes his mind and refuses to cooperate further.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the current legal position?

options.A: Tanya is the legal owner of the property because the deed has been validly executed and delivered.
options.B: Tanya has no legal or equitable interest in the property because Farid has withdrawn his consent.
options.C: Tanya is not yet the legal owner because, for registered land, legal title does not transfer until registration, but she has an equitable interest arising from the contract.
options.D: Tanya is the legal owner because exchange of contracts is the point at which legal title passes.
