options.E: Tanya has an equitable interest but only if she has paid the full purchase price, not merely a deposit.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 27(1) LRA 2002, for registered land, legal title does not transfer until registration has taken place. Since the deed has not been submitted for registration, Tanya is not yet the legal owner. However, the binding contract for sale (compliant with s 2 LP(MP)A 1989) gives Tanya an equitable interest in the land — an estate contract — under the principle in Walsh v Lonsdale. A: Wrong because for registered land the deed alone is insufficient to transfer legal title; registration under s 27(1) LRA 2002 is required. B: Wrong because Farid cannot unilaterally withdraw from a binding contract exchanged in accordance with s 2 LP(MP)A 1989; Tanya has an enforceable equitable interest and could seek specific performance. D: Wrong because exchange of contracts creates a binding obligation and an equitable interest, not legal title; legal title passes on registration (registered land) or on execution of the deed (unregistered land). E: Wrong because the equitable interest under an estate contract arises from the existence of a valid contract and the availability of specific performance, not from payment of the full purchase price.

questions.scenario: Nadia owns a shop in a town centre. Her neighbour, Greg, installs a large metal advertising sign on the side of his building. The sign protrudes approximately 30 centimetres into the airspace directly above Nadia's shop roof. Nadia's use of her roof is unaffected.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Greg has committed a trespass?

options.A: Greg has not committed a trespass because the sign does not touch Nadia's building and causes no physical damage.
options.B: Greg has committed a trespass, but only if Nadia can demonstrate financial loss arising from the overhang.
options.C: Greg has not committed a trespass because Nadia's ordinary use and enjoyment of the land is unaffected.
options.D: Greg has committed a trespass because any incursion into the lower airspace of a neighbouring property constitutes a trespass regardless of whether damage is caused.
options.E: Greg has not committed a trespass because advertising signs are exempt from trespass claims under the Civil Aviation Act.

questions.explanation: D is correct. If a structure overhangs into a property such that it is in the lower airspace, it is a trespass regardless of whether damage is caused. This was established in Kelsen v Imperial Tobacco, where an injunction was granted for removal of an advertising sign projecting into airspace above a shop by only a few inches. A: Wrong because physical contact with the building and physical damage are not required for a trespass in the lower airspace; any incursion into the lower airspace is sufficient. C: Wrong because the test for trespass in the lower airspace does not depend on whether the landowner's use is actually affected; the mere intrusion is actionable. B: Wrong because trespass is actionable per se — financial loss does not need to be demonstrated. E: Wrong because the Civil Aviation Act provides immunity from trespass for innocent flight of aircraft, not for fixed structures such as advertising signs.

questions.scenario: Wendy is buying a registered freehold property from Simon. She reviews the official copies of the title from the Land Registry. She notices an entry in the charges register recording a restrictive covenant in favour of a neighbouring property. Her solicitor tells her there is also a short lease of less than seven years that does not appear on the register.
questions.stem: Which of the following best explains why the short lease does not appear on the register but may still bind Wendy?

options.A: The short lease does not bind Wendy because all interests must be registered to be enforceable against a purchaser of registered land.
options.B: The short lease does not appear on the register because it is an equitable interest that should have been protected by a notice under s 32 LRA 2002.
options.C: The short lease binds Wendy only if she has actual knowledge of its existence at the time of the transfer.
options.D: The short lease does not appear on the register because it has been overreached by the payment of the purchase price to Simon.
options.E: The short lease does not appear on the register because it is an overriding interest under Schedule 3, paragraph 1 LRA 2002, binding Wendy despite non-registration.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Under Schedule 3, paragraph 1 LRA 2002, a legal lease granted for a term not exceeding seven years is an overriding interest. It binds a purchaser even though it does not appear on the register. This is because there are too many short leases in existence to make registration practical, and it is unreasonable to expect short-term tenants to register. A: Wrong because not all interests must be registered to bind a purchaser; overriding interests are a recognised category of interest that binds without registration. B: Wrong because a legal lease of seven years or less is not an equitable interest requiring protection by notice; it is a legal interest that takes effect as an overriding interest. D: Wrong because overreaching applies to beneficial interests in a trust of land, not to legal leases. C: Wrong because overriding interests bind a purchaser regardless of the purchaser's knowledge; actual knowledge is not required for Schedule 3, paragraph 1 interests.

questions.scenario: Rajesh mortgages the freehold of his commercial warehouse to Greenfield Bank. The mortgage deed includes a clause postponing the legal date for redemption by 25 years from the date of the loan. In return, Rajesh receives a favourable interest rate well below the market rate.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the postponement clause is likely to be upheld?

options.A: The clause will be struck out because any postponement of the legal date for redemption is a clog on the equity of redemption.
options.B: The clause will be upheld because the property is a freehold estate and Rajesh received a clear advantage in the form of a favourable interest rate.
options.C: The clause will be struck out because a 25-year postponement prevents Rajesh from ever redeeming the mortgage.
options.D: The clause will be upheld, but only because the mortgage relates to a commercial rather than a domestic property.
options.E: The clause will be struck out because equity requires that all mortgages must be redeemable within a maximum of 10 years.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Following Knightsbridge Estates Trust Ltd v Byrne, a postponement of the legal date for redemption over a freehold estate was upheld because the borrower would eventually get back exactly what had been mortgaged (freehold estates are enduring and rarely lose value) and the borrower had received a favourable interest rate in return. A: Wrong because not every postponement is void; the court examines whether the clause is a clog or fetter in the particular circumstances, considering the nature of the estate and whether the borrower gets something in return. C: Wrong because a 25-year postponement does not prevent redemption altogether — it merely delays it. The mortgage remains redeemable, and the borrower will get back a freehold of the same value. D: Wrong because while the commercial nature of the property is a relevant factor, the key reasons are that the estate is freehold (an enduring asset) and Rajesh received a clear advantage; commercial context alone is not determinative. E: Wrong because there is no fixed maximum period for a postponement clause in law; the court assesses each case on its facts.

questions.scenario: Denise, a vulnerable elderly woman with limited financial understanding, is threatened with eviction by her landlord. Her landlord offers to lend her the money to purchase the property on a mortgage. The mortgage deed does not state the interest rate clearly, but it works out at approximately 35%. Denise had no independent legal advice.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the likely outcome if Denise challenges the interest rate?

options.A: The term will be struck out as unconscionable because there is a clear imbalance of bargaining power, the borrower was exploited, and the rate far exceeds what is reasonable in the circumstances.
options.B: The term will be upheld because borrowers are free to enter into whatever bargain they choose and the court will not interfere with private agreements.
options.C: The term will be upheld because the rate was not explicitly labelled as a penalty in the mortgage deed.
options.D: The term will be struck out solely because the interest rate exceeds the Bank of England base rate.
options.E: The term will be upheld because the lender is entitled to charge whatever rate it wishes as a condition of lending.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Following Cityland and Property Holdings Ltd v Dabrah, the court will strike out an unconscionable interest rate where there is a clear imbalance of bargaining power and the borrower has been exploited. In Cityland, similar circumstances — a tenant threatened with eviction, limited means, no independent advice, and an excessively high rate — led the court to reduce the rate. The test from Multiservice Bookbinding is whether the term was imposed in a 'morally reprehensible manner'. B: Wrong because the court has equitable jurisdiction to intervene with unconscionable terms; freedom of contract does not override this jurisdiction. C: Wrong because the label given to a term is irrelevant; the court examines the substance of the term and the circumstances in which it was imposed. D: Wrong because the Bank of England base rate is not the relevant benchmark; the court considers the totality of the circumstances including bargaining power, not merely the rate in isolation. E: Wrong because the lender's right to set its rate is not unlimited; equity of redemption protects borrowers from unconscionable terms.

questions.scenario: Ahmed wants to expand his business and asks his wife, Fatima, to agree to use their jointly owned home as security for a loan from Northgate Bank. Fatima trusts Ahmed completely and agrees without fully understanding the risks. The loan application states the purpose is to fund Ahmed's sole business venture. Northgate Bank does not take any steps before proceeding with the loan.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the position if Fatima later claims undue influence?

options.A: The bank will be protected because husband and wife relationships carry an irrebuttable presumption of undue influence, meaning Fatima cannot raise the claim.
options.B: Fatima's claim will fail because the loan was made to both Ahmed and Fatima jointly for their mutual benefit.
options.C: The mortgage may be unenforceable against Fatima because the bank was put on inquiry (the loan was not for joint benefit) and failed to follow the Etridge guidelines.
options.D: The mortgage will be enforceable against Fatima because undue influence only applies where there has been an overt act of coercion or threat.
options.E: Fatima's claim will succeed automatically because she did not receive independent legal advice, regardless of whether the bank was put on inquiry.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under RBS v Etridge (No 2), a bank is put on inquiry whenever one party in a non-commercial setting is standing as surety for the other party. Here, the loan is for Ahmed's sole business venture, not for joint benefit. The bank should have followed the Etridge guidelines: writing to Fatima, requiring her to obtain independent legal advice, and not proceeding until confirmation from the solicitor was received. Failure to follow these steps means the bank cannot protect itself from a claim of undue influence, and the mortgage may be unenforceable against Fatima. A: Wrong because husband and wife relationships do not carry an irrebuttable presumption of undue influence; there is no presumption at all for spouses — influence must be positively shown. B: Wrong because the loan was stated to be for Ahmed's sole business venture, not for joint benefit. Under CIBC Mortgages v Pitt, the bank is not put on inquiry only where the loan is for joint benefit. C is the correct analysis. D: Wrong because undue influence is not limited to overt coercion; Etridge recognises that taking advantage of influence or ascendancy in a relationship also constitutes undue influence. E: Wrong because the claim does not succeed automatically; the bank's liability depends on whether it was put on inquiry and failed to take steps, and whether undue influence is actually established on the facts.

questions.scenario: Carla has a legal mortgage over her registered property with Bridgewater Finance. Her mortgage is a capital repayment mortgage. She has missed three monthly payments and is now four months in arrears. No express power of sale is included in the mortgage deed.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the lender's statutory power of sale has arisen and become exercisable?

options.A: The power of sale has arisen and is exercisable because Carla has missed three monthly payments, satisfying both the s 101 and s 103 LPA 1925 requirements.
options.B: The power of sale has not arisen because no express power of sale is included in the mortgage deed.
options.C: The power of sale has arisen under s 101 LPA 1925 because mortgage money has become due, but it is not yet exercisable because none of the s 103 criteria have been met.
options.D: The power of sale has arisen and is exercisable because mortgage money has become due under s 101, and interest has been in arrears for at least two months under s 103 LPA 1925.
options.E: The power of sale cannot be exercised until the lender has obtained a court order for possession.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under s 101(1)(i) LPA 1925, the statutory power of sale arises when mortgage money has become due. For a capital repayment mortgage, following Payne v Cardiff, the power arises as soon as one portion of capital is due. Since Carla has missed payments, mortgage money has become due. The power becomes exercisable when at least one of the s 103 criteria is met. Here, interest has been unpaid and in arrears for at least two months (she is four months in arrears), satisfying s 103. C: Wrong because while the conclusion is correct, the reasoning conflates the 'arisen' and 'exercisable' tests; the power arises under s 101 when mortgage money becomes due, and becomes exercisable under s 103 when a criterion is met — the number of missed payments is relevant to s 103, not s 101. B: Wrong because s 101(1)(i) implies a statutory power of sale into the mortgage unless it is expressly excluded; the absence of an express power does not prevent the statutory power from applying. A: Wrong because the s 103 criteria have been met — interest has been in arrears for more than two months, which is one of the s 103 grounds making the power exercisable. E: Wrong because the power of sale is a right of the lender that does not require a court order; it is the right to possession that typically involves court proceedings in practice, not the power of sale itself.

questions.scenario: Elena owns a registered freehold property. She grants a legal mortgage to Alpha Bank, which is registered on 5 March. She then grants a second legal mortgage to Beta Bank, which is registered on 12 March. Both mortgages are properly created by deed.
questions.stem: If Elena defaults and the property is sold, which of the following best describes how priority between the two mortgages is determined?

options.A: Priority is determined by the date on which the mortgage deeds were signed by Elena.
options.B: Beta Bank has priority because the most recent mortgage always takes precedence over earlier ones.
options.C: Alpha Bank and Beta Bank share equally in the proceeds because both are legal mortgages over the same property.
options.D: Priority depends on which bank first served notice on Elena demanding repayment.
options.E: Priority is determined by the order in which the mortgages are entered on the register, so Alpha Bank has priority.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Under s 48 LRA 2002, priority between registered charges depends on the order in which they are entered on the register, regardless of the order in which the mortgage deeds were signed. Alpha Bank was registered first on 5 March and therefore has priority over Beta Bank, which was registered on 12 March. A: Wrong because the date of signing is irrelevant to priority between registered charges; it is the order of registration that determines priority under s 48 LRA 2002. C: Wrong because registered charges do not share equally; the first registered charge takes priority and is paid in full before any surplus goes to the second charge. D: Wrong because service of a demand for repayment has no bearing on priority between mortgages; priority is determined by registration order. B: Wrong because the most recent mortgage does not take precedence; the opposite is true — the first registered mortgage has priority.

questions.scenario: Rosalind owns a large garden adjoining her home. She sells her home to Patrick but retains the garden. Rosalind purports to grant Patrick a right to sit in the garden and enjoy the view of the distant hills whenever he wishes.
questions.stem: Which of the following is the most likely reason why this right may fail to qualify as an easement?

options.A: The right is not capable of forming the subject-matter of a deed because it is too vague to be enforced with reasonable exactness.
options.B: There is no dominant and servient tenement.
options.C: The right does not accommodate the dominant tenement because it only provides a personal benefit to Patrick.
options.D: The dominant and servient tenements are not in sufficiently close proximity.
options.E: The right has not been judicially recognised as being capable of being an easement.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under the Re Ellenborough Park test, a right must lie in grant, which requires it to be capable of reasonably exact description. A right to 'enjoy the view' is too vague, as demonstrated in William Aldreds Case where a scenic view was rejected as being incapable of precise definition. The court would not know exactly what was to be enforced. B: Wrong because there is a dominant tenement (Patrick's home) and a servient tenement (Rosalind's garden), so this limb of the test is satisfied. C: Wrong because a right to use a neighbouring garden for leisure purposes can accommodate the dominant tenement (as in Re Ellenborough Park itself); the problem here is the vagueness of the right, not whether it benefits the land. D: Wrong because the properties are adjoining, so proximity is not an issue. E: Wrong because the use of leisure facilities and recreational purposes has been recognised as capable of being an easement in Re Ellenborough Park and Regency Villas; the problem is the vagueness of the 'view' element, not a failure of judicial recognition.

questions.scenario: Nathan owns a plot of land adjacent to Julia's property. Julia has been parking her car in a marked space on Nathan's land every day for the past five years. Nathan's land is a large car park with 50 spaces, and Nathan continues to use the remaining spaces freely. Julia claims an easement by prescription.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the position regarding exclusive possession as a disqualifying factor?

options.A: Julia's right is disqualified because any right to park on another's land necessarily amounts to exclusive possession.
options.B: Julia's right is not disqualified because, applying Kettel v Bloomfold, Nathan retains possession and control of the land and can do anything except interfere with Julia's parking.
options.C: Julia's right is disqualified because Nathan cannot make any reasonable use of the space while Julia's car is parked there, applying the ouster principle from Batchelor v Marlow.
options.D: Julia's right is disqualified because she has been using the space every day, which amounts to permanent occupation.
options.E: Julia's right is not disqualified because the Moncrieff v Jamieson test has replaced the ouster principle entirely in English law.

questions.explanation: B is correct. In Kettel v Bloomfold, the court applied the ouster test in a Moncrieff way, finding that where the servient owner retains control of the space and can do anything except interfere with the parking, the right is not disqualified by exclusive possession. Nathan has a large car park and continues to use the remaining spaces, retaining possession and control of the land. A: Wrong because parking has been recognised as capable of being an easement (Kettel v Bloomfold); it does not necessarily amount to exclusive possession. C: Wrong because the ouster principle asks whether the servient owner is left with no reasonable use of the land; here Nathan has 49 other spaces and retains full use of the car park, so reasonable use is preserved. D: Wrong because daily use of a parking space does not equate to permanent occupation or exclusive possession; the servient owner still retains control of the space and the wider land. E: Wrong because Moncrieff v Jamieson is a Scottish case and not binding in English law; the ouster principle from Batchelor v Marlow remains binding, though it may be applied in a Moncrieff way as in Kettel.

questions.scenario: For the past 25 years, Derek has used a path across Sylvia's registered freehold land to access his own property. Derek has always used the path openly during daylight hours. Sylvia has been aware of his use throughout but has never given express permission or objected. Both Derek and Sylvia are freehold owners.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Derek can claim an easement by prescription?

options.A: Derek cannot claim an easement by prescription because Sylvia has tolerated his use, which amounts to implied permission.
options.B: Derek cannot claim an easement by prescription because he has only used the path during daylight hours, which is not continuous use.
options.C: Derek cannot claim an easement by prescription because prescription requires use since time immemorial.
options.D: Derek can claim an easement by prescription because the use has been continuous, open, and without force, secrecy, or permission for more than 20 years.
options.E: Derek can claim an easement by prescription, but only if he can produce written evidence of his use from a 20-year period.

questions.explanation: D is correct. The basic criteria for prescription are satisfied: Derek's use has been continuous (reasonably regular for over 20 years), by a freeholder against a freeholder, and 'as of right' — without force (nec vi), without secrecy (nec clam), and without permission (nec precario). Sylvia's awareness and toleration do not amount to permission. A: Wrong because toleration of use is not the same as permission; this distinction is critical in prescription law. Permission means the servient owner has actively granted consent, not merely passively allowed the use to continue. D is the correct analysis. C: Wrong because while prescription at common law theoretically requires use since time immemorial, the doctrine of Lost Modern Grant and the Prescription Act 1832 provide alternative routes that require only 20 years of qualifying use. B: Wrong because 'continuous' use does not mean constant or 24-hour use; it means 'reasonably regular use', as established in Mills v Silver. Using a path during daylight hours is reasonably regular. E: Wrong because prescription does not require written evidence; it is established through evidence of long use, which can be oral testimony or other evidence demonstrating the pattern of use.

questions.scenario: Louise owns a large plot of land and sells the northern half to Craig. In the transfer deed, Louise reserves a right of way over the northern half to access a shed on the southern half she retains. Two years later, Louise begins using the right of way to access a newly built garage, which requires a different route across Craig's land.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Louise can extend her reserved right of way to access the new garage?

options.A: Louise can extend the right because she originally owned the whole plot and should be entitled to full use of the northern half.
options.B: Louise can extend the right because the original reservation should be interpreted broadly to include any reasonable access.
options.C: Louise cannot extend the right because reservations are strictly construed according to the transfer deed, and extending it would derogate from the grant made to Craig.
options.D: Louise cannot extend the right because reservations can never be implied into a transfer deed.
options.E: Louise can extend the right because the garage enhances the value of the dominant land.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Reservations are strictly construed against the person reserving, as they were in the position to reserve exactly what was required and are assumed to have done so. Following Cordell v Second Clanfield Properties, any attempt to extend the right beyond what was expressly reserved will fail, because interpreting the right more widely would derogate from the grant made to Craig, giving him lesser use of his land than originally envisaged. A: Wrong because original ownership of the whole plot is irrelevant once the land has been divided; Louise's rights are limited to what was expressly reserved. B: Wrong because reservations are not interpreted broadly; the opposite is true — they are strictly construed according to the transfer deed. D: Wrong because reservations can be implied in certain circumstances (eg by necessity or common intention), although this is difficult; the statement that they can 'never' be implied is too absolute. E: Wrong because the fact that the garage enhances the value of Louise's land does not justify extending a reservation beyond its terms; the strict construction principle applies regardless of benefit to the dominant land.

questions.scenario: Martin owns a flat above a shop owned by Evelyn. Martin claims he has an easement requiring Evelyn to maintain the central heating boiler in her shop, which supplies hot water to Martin's flat. Evelyn argues this cannot be an easement.
questions.stem: Which of the following best explains why this right is unlikely to be an easement?

options.A: The right fails because it does not accommodate the dominant tenement.
options.B: The right fails because it has not been judicially recognised as a type of easement.
options.C: The right fails because the dominant and servient tenements are not owned by different people.
options.D: The right fails because there is no dominant and servient tenement.
options.E: The right fails because it requires the servient owner to incur expenditure, which is inconsistent with the nature of an easement.

questions.explanation: E is correct. If the exercise of a right requires expenditure from the servient owner, the right is disqualified from being an easement. This was established in Regis Property Co Ltd v Redman, where a right to a supply of hot water was not held to be an easement because the servient owner was forced to spend money to supply it. A positive obligation to maintain a boiler is inconsistent with the concept of an easement. A: Wrong because the right to hot water does benefit Martin's flat and therefore could accommodate the dominant tenement; the disqualifying factor is the expenditure requirement, not the accommodation test. D: Wrong because supply of water has been considered in the context of easements (Rance v Elvin); the issue is the obligation on the servient owner to maintain equipment at their own expense. C: Wrong because there are identifiable dominant (Martin's flat) and servient (Evelyn's shop) tenements. B: Wrong because Martin and Evelyn are different people, so diversity of ownership is satisfied.

questions.scenario: Callum buys a plot of farmland from Heather. The plot is entirely surrounded by Heather's retained land with no access to any public road. The transfer deed makes no mention of a right of way.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Callum can claim an implied easement of necessity?

options.A: Callum can claim an implied easement of necessity because the land is landlocked and without a right of way no use of the land can be made.
options.B: Callum cannot claim an implied easement because an easement of necessity can only be reserved, not granted.
options.C: Callum can claim an implied easement for a right of way and also for drainage and electricity supply, because all three are necessary for practical use of the land.
options.D: Callum cannot claim an implied easement because the transfer deed is silent and implied easements are not recognised in English law.
options.E: Callum can claim an implied easement of necessity, but only if he can show that Heather intended to grant him a right of way at the time of the sale.

questions.explanation: A is correct. An easement of necessity will be implied where the land is landlocked and the easement is essential for any use of the dominant land. The only easement that can be implied by necessity is a right of way to otherwise landlocked land, as established in Pryce v McGuinness. Callum's plot is entirely surrounded by Heather's land with no access to any road, so it is landlocked and a right of way by necessity will be implied into the transfer deed. B: Wrong because easements of necessity can be implied as grants (to the buyer of the dominant land) as well as reservations; this is a grant situation where Callum, the buyer, benefits. C: Wrong because Pryce v McGuinness established that easements for drainage, sewage, and electricity supply are highly advantageous but not essential for any use of the land; only a right of way to landlocked land qualifies as an easement of necessity. D: Wrong because implied easements are well-recognised in English law through several methods including necessity, common intention, Wheeldon v Burrows, and s 62 LPA 1925. E: Wrong because an easement of necessity does not depend on showing the seller's intention to grant it; it arises because the land would be entirely unusable without the right of way.

questions.scenario: Sandra owns a house with a large garden. She regularly walks across the back of the garden to reach a greenhouse, using a clearly worn gravel path. Sandra sells the house (retaining the garden) to Tom. The transfer deed does not mention the gravel path. Tom now wishes to use the path to access the greenhouse, which is on Sandra's retained land.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Tom can claim an implied easement under the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows?

options.A: Tom can claim an implied easement because Sandra used the path as a quasi-easement before the sale, and the path is continuous and apparent.
options.B: Tom cannot claim an implied easement because the right of way is not necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of his land, since the greenhouse is on Sandra's retained land, not on Tom's land.
options.C: Tom cannot claim an implied easement because the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows only applies to reservations, not grants.
options.D: Tom can claim an implied easement because s 62 LPA 1925 automatically upgrades all informal rights into legal easements on any conveyance.
options.E: Tom cannot claim an implied easement because quasi-easements can never become full easements after a division of land.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under Wheeldon v Burrows, the quasi-easement must be necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the dominant land — that is, the land sold to Tom (the house). The greenhouse is on Sandra's retained land, not Tom's. A right of way to access a building on someone else's land does not enhance Tom's land; it would benefit Sandra's land. The rule in Wheeldon v Burrows implies easements that benefit the land sold, not the land retained. A: Wrong because although Sandra did use the path as a quasi-easement and it may be continuous and apparent, the requirement that the right must be necessary for the reasonable enjoyment of the dominant land (Tom's house) is not met, since the greenhouse is not on Tom's land. C: Wrong because Wheeldon v Burrows applies only to grants, not reservations — but Tom's claim still fails on other grounds. D: Wrong because s 62 LPA 1925 does not automatically upgrade all informal rights; specific requirements must be met, and in any event s 62 does not assist Tom here because the right does not benefit his land. E: Wrong because quasi-easements can become full easements after division of land — that is precisely what Wheeldon v Burrows and s 62 LPA 1925 contemplate.

questions.scenario: Fiona leases a ground-floor flat to Gerald. As a favour, she gives Gerald informal permission to store his bicycle in a shed on an adjoining plot she also owns. When the lease expires, Fiona grants Gerald a new legal lease of the same flat. The new lease does not mention the shed.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Gerald now has a legal easement to use the shed?

options.A: Gerald does not have a legal easement because informal permissions can never become easements.
options.B: Gerald does not have a legal easement because s 62 LPA 1925 only applies to freehold conveyances, not leases.
options.C: Gerald has a legal easement because s 62 LPA 1925 operates to upgrade the informal permission to use the shed into a legal easement, implied into the new lease.
options.D: Gerald has a legal easement, but only if Fiona expressly consented to the upgrade in the new lease.
options.E: Gerald does not have a legal easement because storage rights cannot be easements.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under the upgrade effect of s 62 LPA 1925, as illustrated in Wright v Macadam, an informal permission granted to an occupier of the dominant land can be upgraded into a full legal easement when a conveyance (including a legal lease) of the dominant land takes place. The requirements are met: there was prior diversity of occupation (Fiona occupied the adjoining plot, Gerald occupied the flat), an informal permission was granted, and a conveyance (the new legal lease) has been executed. The easement is implied into the new lease and takes its legal status from the legal lease. A: Wrong because s 62 LPA 1925 specifically operates to upgrade informal permissions into legal easements on a conveyance, as established in Wright v Macadam. B: Wrong because s 62 LPA 1925 applies to any 'conveyance', which includes a legal lease as well as a freehold transfer. D: Wrong because s 62 operates automatically by virtue of the statute; no express consent from the grantor is required. E: Wrong because storage has been judicially recognised as capable of being an easement in Wright v Macadam.

questions.scenario: Imogen grants Kwame a right to use a driveway across her registered freehold land for access to his property. The right is granted 'until the new bypass road is completed'. The grant is made in a document that is in writing and signed by both parties, but it is not executed as a deed.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the status of Kwame's right?

options.A: The right is a legal easement because it satisfies the formality requirements for a legal interest.
options.B: The right is void because it has not been created by deed.
options.C: The right is an equitable easement recognised as an estate contract under s 2 LP(MP)A 1989.
options.D: The right is an inherently equitable easement because it is granted for an uncertain term, and it satisfies the minimum formalities under s 53(1)(a) LPA 1925.
options.E: The right is a legal easement because all easements over freehold land are automatically legal.

questions.explanation: D is correct. An easement granted for an uncertain term (here, 'until the new bypass road is completed') cannot fall within the definition of a legal easement under s 1(2)(a) LPA 1925, which requires the easement to be for a term equivalent to a freehold or leasehold estate. It is therefore 'inherently equitable'. The minimum formalities for an inherently equitable easement are set out in s 53(1)(a) LPA 1925: in writing and signed by both parties. These formalities are satisfied. E: Wrong because a legal easement must be granted for a term equivalent to a freehold or leasehold estate under s 1(2)(a) LPA 1925 and must be created by deed; neither requirement is met here. A: Wrong because although the absence of a deed prevents a legal easement, the right is not void; it can take effect as an equitable easement if the appropriate formalities are met. C: Wrong because the estate contract route under s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 applies to easements that fall within the definition of a legal easement but fail to comply with the legal formalities (eg no deed or no registration). An easement for an uncertain term is inherently equitable and cannot be a 'failed legal easement' recognised as an estate contract. B: Wrong because easements are not automatically legal; they must be granted for the correct duration and comply with the legal formalities to be legal.

questions.scenario: Vincent holds an express equitable easement over Miriam's registered freehold land. Vincent has protected the easement by entry of a notice on the charges register of Miriam's title under s 32 LRA 2002. Miriam now sells her land to Sophie for valuable consideration.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the easement is binding on Sophie?

options.A: The easement is not binding on Sophie because equitable easements can never bind a purchaser for value of registered land.
options.B: The easement is binding on Sophie because equitable easements are overriding interests under Schedule 3 LRA 2002.
options.C: The easement is binding on Sophie only if she had actual knowledge of it at the time of the transfer.
options.D: The easement is not binding on Sophie because only legal easements can be protected by a notice.
options.E: The easement is binding on Sophie because it has been protected by entry of a notice under s 32 LRA 2002, making it binding under s 29(2) LRA 2002.

questions.explanation: E is correct. An express equitable easement that has been protected by entry of a notice on the charges register of the servient land under s 32 LRA 2002 will be binding on a purchaser for valuable consideration under s 29(2) LRA 2002. Vincent has taken the correct step to protect his equitable easement. A: Wrong because equitable easements can bind a purchaser for value if they have been properly protected by a notice on the charges register. E is the correct analysis. D: Wrong because once the easement is protected by a notice, it binds the purchaser regardless of their actual knowledge; the registration system replaces the need for actual notice. C: Wrong because s 32 LRA 2002 expressly provides for equitable interests (including equitable easements) to be protected by entry of a notice. B: Wrong because equitable easements are not overriding interests under Schedule 3 LRA 2002; Schedule 3, paragraph 3 applies only to implied legal easements. Express equitable easements must be protected by notice.

questions.scenario: David sells part of his freehold land to Elena. The transfer deed contains the following covenant: 'The buyer covenants with the seller to keep the boundary fence between the two properties in good repair at all times.'
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the nature of this covenant?

options.A: The covenant is positive because compliance requires Elena to expend money, effort, or time to maintain the fence.
options.B: The covenant is restrictive because it restricts Elena from allowing the fence to fall into disrepair.
options.C: The covenant is restrictive because it relates to the physical condition of the land.
options.D: The covenant is positive, but it can be enforced against a successor covenantor in equity under Tulk v Moxhay.
options.E: The covenant is neither positive nor restrictive; it is a mixed covenant that must be split into separate obligations.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Applying the 'hand in pocket' test from Haywood v Brunswick PBBS, the substance of the obligation requires Elena to spend money, effort, or time to keep the fence in good repair. This makes it a positive covenant regardless of how it is worded. The test looks at the substance, not the form. E: Wrong because the substance of the obligation is not to refrain from doing something, but to actively maintain the fence; this requires expenditure and is therefore positive. C: Wrong because the fact that a covenant relates to the physical condition of the land does not make it restrictive; the nature depends on whether compliance requires expenditure. D: Wrong because positive covenants cannot be enforced against a successor covenantor in equity; Haywood v Brunswick PBBS established that only restrictive covenants can run with the land in equity under the Tulk v Moxhay rules. B: Wrong because the covenant contains a single obligation (to keep the fence in good repair) that is clearly positive in substance; there is no restrictive element to separate out.

questions.scenario: Amy sells part of her registered freehold to Ben, who covenants not to use the land for any commercial purpose. The covenant is protected by a notice on the charges register of Ben's title. Ben later sells his land to Claire.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the burden of the covenant has passed to Claire?

options.A: The burden has not passed to Claire because the burden of a covenant can never pass to a successor under any circumstances.
options.B: The burden has passed to Claire because all covenants contained in a transfer deed automatically bind successors.
options.C: The burden has passed to Claire at common law because Amy expressly included it in the transfer deed.
options.D: The burden has passed to Claire in equity because the covenant is restrictive, it accommodates Amy's dominant land, there is intention for the burden to run (implied by s 79 LPA 1925), and Claire has notice via the registered notice.
options.E: The burden has not passed to Claire because the covenant was not annexed to Amy's land.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under the equitable rules from Tulk v Moxhay, the burden of a restrictive covenant can pass to a successor if four conditions are met: (1) the covenant must be restrictive — a promise not to use for commercial purposes is restrictive; (2) the covenant must accommodate the dominant tenement — it must benefit Amy's retained land; (3) there must be intention for the burden to run — implied by s 79 LPA 1925 unless excluded; (4) the successor must have notice — satisfied here because the covenant is protected by a notice on the charges register under s 32 LRA 2002. C: Wrong because while the general rule is that the burden of a covenant does not pass at common law, the Tulk v Moxhay equitable exception allows the burden of restrictive covenants to pass if the conditions are met. B: Wrong because covenants do not automatically bind successors; specific conditions must be satisfied depending on whether the equitable or common law rules apply. E: Wrong because the burden of a covenant does not pass to successors at common law regardless of how it is expressed; common law only allows the benefit to pass. A: Wrong because annexation relates to passing the benefit of a covenant, not the burden; the burden passes under Tulk v Moxhay if the four conditions are satisfied.

questions.scenario: In 1995, George sold part of his freehold land to Helen. Helen covenanted not to build above two storeys on the land. The covenant does not contain any express words annexing the benefit to George's retained land, nor does it expressly exclude statutory annexation. George later sells his retained land to Isaac.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the benefit of the covenant has passed to Isaac?

options.A: The benefit has not passed because there are no express words of annexation in the covenant.
options.B: The benefit has passed by statutory annexation under s 78 LPA 1925, as interpreted in Federated Homes v Mill Lodge, because the covenant relates to George's land and statutory annexation has not been expressly excluded.
options.C: The benefit has passed only if Isaac can produce a written assignment of the benefit from George.
options.D: The benefit has not passed because statutory annexation was abolished by the LRA 2002.
options.E: The benefit has passed because the benefit of all covenants passes automatically regardless of any statutory provision.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under s 78(1) LPA 1925, as interpreted in Federated Homes v Mill Lodge Properties, a covenant relating to the covenantee's land is deemed to be made with the covenantee and successors in title of the land intended to be benefitted. This makes annexation automatic unless expressly excluded in the wording of the covenant or impliedly excluded by providing an alternative method for passing the benefit. Since neither exclusion applies here, the benefit is automatically annexed to George's retained land and passes to Isaac on the sale. A: Wrong because the absence of express words of annexation does not prevent the benefit from passing; s 78 LPA 1925 provides for statutory annexation in the absence of express annexation. D: Wrong because assignment is only necessary where the benefit has not been annexed at the outset; since statutory annexation applies here, no separate assignment is required. E: Wrong because statutory annexation under s 78 LPA 1925 was not abolished by the LRA 2002; it remains good law. C: Wrong because the benefit does not pass entirely automatically in all circumstances; it must pass by one of the recognised methods (annexation, assignment, or building scheme), though statutory annexation makes the process largely automatic in practice.

questions.scenario: Meadowbrook Developments Ltd builds a new housing estate of 40 houses. All houses are sold subject to the same restrictive covenants, including a covenant not to erect satellite dishes on the front of the properties. Rachel, who owns Plot 12, notices that Kevin, who owns Plot 27, has erected a satellite dish on his front wall.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how Rachel can enforce the covenant against Kevin?

options.A: Rachel cannot enforce the covenant because she was not the original covenantee.
options.B: Rachel can enforce the covenant, but only by suing Meadowbrook Developments as the original covenantee.
options.C: Rachel can enforce the covenant only if she can show that the benefit was expressly assigned to her by Meadowbrook Developments.
options.D: Rachel cannot enforce the covenant because the burden of a restrictive covenant cannot pass between owners of land bought from the same seller.
options.E: Rachel can enforce the covenant because a building scheme exists, meaning the covenants are treated as mutually enforceable by-laws binding all owners on the estate.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Where the conditions of a building scheme are met (as set out in Elliston v Reacher), the covenants are treated as a set of mutually enforceable by-laws binding all owners on the estate. It is not necessary for Rachel to show specifically that the benefit of a particular covenant has passed to her. The conditions — all buyers bought from the same seller, the estate was divided into plots, covenants were intended to benefit all plots, and each buyer bought on that understanding — appear to be satisfied here. B: Wrong because under a building scheme, it is not necessary for the claimant to be the original covenantee; the scheme creates mutual enforceability among all plot owners. C: Wrong because the purpose of a building scheme is to avoid the need to trace the benefit through express assignment; the scheme creates a network of mutually enforceable obligations. D: Wrong because the burden of a restrictive covenant can pass to successors in equity under Tulk v Moxhay, and a building scheme specifically facilitates enforcement between owners who bought from the same seller. A: Wrong because under a building scheme, Rachel can enforce the covenant directly against Kevin in her own right, without involving the original covenantee.

questions.scenario: A restrictive covenant imposed in 1960 prevents any commercial use of a plot of land in what was then a quiet residential street. The surrounding area has since been redeveloped and is now a busy commercial district with shops, offices, and restaurants on every side. The current owner of the burdened land wishes to open a café.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the most appropriate ground for applying to discharge or modify the covenant?

options.A: S 84(1)(b) LPA 1925: the dominant owners have expressly agreed to discharge the covenant.
options.B: S 84(1)(c) LPA 1925: the dominant owners will not suffer injury from the discharge.
options.C: S 84(1)(a) LPA 1925: the covenant is obsolete due to changes in the character of the neighbourhood.
options.D: The covenant can be discharged by the county court under its general equitable jurisdiction.
options.E: The covenant has been automatically discharged because it is more than 50 years old.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 84(1)(a) LPA 1925, the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) has the power to discharge or modify a restrictive covenant where it has become obsolete due to changes in the character of the property or neighbourhood. The surrounding area has been redeveloped into a busy commercial district, which represents a significant change in character from the quiet residential street of 1960. A: Wrong because there is no indication that the dominant owners have expressly agreed to discharge; this ground (s 84(1)(b)) requires active consent from the dominant owners. B: Wrong because while s 84(1)(c) is a valid ground, it requires showing that the dominant owners will suffer no injury; the question does not address whether the dominant land is still affected, and s 84(1)(a) is a more directly applicable ground on these facts. D: Wrong because applications for discharge or modification of restrictive covenants must be made to the Upper Tribunal (Lands Chamber) under s 84(1) LPA 1925, not the county court. E: Wrong because restrictive covenants do not expire after any fixed period; they last indefinitely unless discharged or modified by one of the recognised methods.

questions.scenario: Leon is the sole registered proprietor of a house. His partner, Maya, contributed 30% of the purchase price but is not named on the title. Maya lives in the house. Leon, without Maya's knowledge, mortgages the house to Pinnacle Bank. The bank pays the mortgage money to Leon alone.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes Maya's position?

options.A: Maya has a beneficial interest under an implied trust which has not been overreached (as the money was paid to only one trustee) and is an overriding interest binding the bank because she is in actual occupation.
options.B: Maya's interest has been overreached because the bank paid the mortgage money to Leon.
options.C: Maya has no interest in the property because she is not named on the legal title.
options.D: Maya's interest is not binding on the bank because she did not register a notice on the charges register.
options.E: Maya's interest is binding on the bank only if the bank had actual knowledge of her contribution to the purchase price.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Maya's contribution to the purchase price gives her a beneficial interest under an implied trust. Under Williams & Glyn's Bank v Boland, where the mortgage money is paid to only one trustee, overreaching does not occur and the beneficial interest remains in the land. Maya's interest is an overriding interest under Schedule 3, paragraph 2 LRA 2002 because she has a proprietary interest and is in actual occupation. The bank is therefore bound. C: Wrong because a person need not be named on the legal title to have a proprietary interest; Maya's contribution to the purchase price gives rise to an implied trust, under which she holds a beneficial (equitable) interest. B: Wrong because overreaching requires payment to at least two trustees under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925; Leon is the sole trustee, so overreaching has not occurred. D: Wrong because beneficial interests in a trust of land cannot be protected by a notice under s 33 LRA 2002; they are protected by restriction or, if the beneficiary is in actual occupation, as overriding interests. E: Wrong because overriding interests bind a purchaser regardless of actual knowledge; Maya's actual occupation is sufficient without the bank needing to know about her contribution.

questions.scenario: Robert and Sarah are registered as joint legal owners of a property. Their friend, Aisha, contributed 20% of the purchase price and has a beneficial interest under an implied trust. Aisha lives in the property. Robert and Sarah sell the property to James, who pays the full purchase price to Robert and Sarah jointly.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Aisha's interest binds James?

options.A: Aisha's interest binds James because she is in actual occupation and has an overriding interest under Schedule 3, paragraph 2 LRA 2002.
options.B: Aisha's interest does not bind James because she is not named on the legal title and therefore has no proprietary interest.
options.C: Aisha's interest binds James because beneficial interests in a trust of land can never be overreached.
options.D: Aisha's interest does not bind James because it has been overreached — the purchase money was paid to two trustees, transferring her interest from the land to the sale proceeds.
options.E: Aisha's interest binds James only if she registered a restriction on the proprietorship register.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925, a purchaser who pays the capital money to at least two trustees will overreach any beneficial interest under a trust. Robert and Sarah are two legal owners (trustees), and James paid the full price to both of them. Aisha's beneficial interest is therefore transferred from the land to the sale proceeds. Following City of London BS v Flegg, overreaching operates even where the beneficiary is in actual occupation. A: Wrong because although Aisha is in actual occupation, overreaching takes priority; where the purchase money has been paid to two trustees, the interest is removed from the land regardless of occupation. C: Wrong because beneficial interests in a trust of land can be overreached — that is precisely the function of the overreaching mechanism under ss 2 and 27 LPA 1925. B: Wrong because Aisha does have a proprietary interest (a beneficial interest under an implied trust arising from her financial contribution); the fact that she is not on the legal title does not negate this. E: Wrong because while a restriction could have been entered, it is not necessary for Aisha's interest to be overreached; overreaching occurs automatically when purchase money is paid to two trustees.

questions.scenario: In 2018, Peter grants Liam an option to purchase his unregistered freehold farm. Liam does not protect the option by registering a land charge. In 2020, Peter sells the farm to his neighbour, Deborah, for £15,000. The farm is valued at £400,000. Deborah knows about the option.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether the option is binding on Deborah?

options.A: The option is binding on Deborah because she has actual knowledge of it.
options.B: The option is binding on Deborah because the sale price of £15,000 is so far below market value that Deborah cannot be a 'purchaser for value'.
options.C: The option is not binding on Deborah because it was not protected by a Class C(IV) land charge, and failure to register makes it void against a purchaser regardless of knowledge.
options.D: The option is not binding on Deborah, but only because she is a neighbour and therefore has a conflict of interest.
options.E: The option is binding on Deborah under the doctrine of notice because she had constructive notice of it.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 4 LCA 1972, an unprotected land charge is void against a purchaser of the land. Following Midland Bank Trust Co Ltd v Green, registration or failure to register is conclusive as to notice — the purchaser's actual knowledge of the interest is irrelevant. The option should have been protected as a Class C(IV) land charge (estate contract). Since Liam failed to register it, the option is void against Deborah even though she knew about it and paid a nominal price. A: Wrong because for interests capable of registration as land charges, actual knowledge is irrelevant; the registration system is conclusive under Midland Bank Trust v Green. B: Wrong because Midland Bank Trust v Green established that the court will not inquire into the adequacy of the consideration; Deborah paid something for the land and therefore qualifies as a 'purchaser'. D: Wrong because the relationship between the parties has no bearing on whether an unprotected land charge is void; the test is purely whether the interest was registered. E: Wrong because the doctrine of notice does not apply to interests that are capable of protection by land charge registration; the land charges system replaces the doctrine of notice for such interests.

questions.scenario: Hannah grants a legal mortgage over her registered freehold property to Westfield Finance. The mortgage is created by a valid deed but Westfield Finance fails to submit the deed for registration at the Land Registry.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the status of Westfield Finance's mortgage?

options.A: The mortgage is a valid legal mortgage because it was created by deed.
options.B: The mortgage does not operate at law because a legal mortgage over registered land is a registrable disposition under s 27(2)(f) LRA 2002 and must be completed by registration.
options.C: The mortgage is void and has no legal effect whatsoever.
options.D: The mortgage is a valid legal mortgage because registration is optional for mortgages.
options.E: The mortgage operates as a legal mortgage for 12 months, after which it becomes void if not registered.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under s 27(2)(f) LRA 2002, the grant of a legal mortgage is a registrable disposition that must be completed by registration. Under s 27(1) LRA 2002, the disposition does not operate at law until registration has been completed. Westfield Finance therefore does not have a legal mortgage. However, the mortgage is not necessarily void — it may take effect as an equitable mortgage if it complies with the relevant formalities. A: Wrong because for registered land, a deed alone is insufficient to create a legal mortgage; registration is required under s 27 LRA 2002. C: Wrong because while the mortgage does not operate at law, it is not necessarily void altogether; it may be recognised in equity as an equitable mortgage. D: Wrong because registration of a legal mortgage over registered land is compulsory, not optional. E: Wrong because there is no 12-month grace period for registration of a mortgage over already registered land; the disposition simply does not operate at law until registration.

questions.scenario: Thomas buys an unregistered freehold property from Catherine. Before completing the purchase, Thomas's solicitor inspects the property and notices signs of occupation by another person. The solicitor does not make any further enquiries. It later transpires that Catherine's sister, Vera, has a beneficial interest in the property under a trust that has not been overreached.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether Vera's interest binds Thomas?

options.A: Vera's interest does not bind Thomas because it was not protected by a land charge.
options.B: Vera's interest does not bind Thomas because beneficial interests under a trust are always overreached on any sale.
options.C: Vera's interest does not bind Thomas because the doctrine of notice has been abolished.
options.D: Vera's interest binds Thomas only if Vera can prove that Thomas personally knew about her interest.
options.E: Vera's interest binds Thomas because he has constructive notice of it — his solicitor noticed signs of occupation and failed to make enquiries, and the solicitor's knowledge is imputed to Thomas.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Beneficial interests under a trust of land over unregistered land cannot be protected by land charge and are therefore governed by the doctrine of notice. Thomas's solicitor noticed signs of occupation but failed to pursue further enquiries. Under the doctrine of constructive notice, a purchaser is fixed with notice of interests they would have discovered had they made the enquiries they ought reasonably to have made. Knowledge of the solicitor (as agent) is imputed to Thomas. Following Kingsnorth Finance v Tizard, imputed constructive notice via a surveyor or solicitor binds the purchaser. B: Wrong because beneficial interests in a trust of land cannot be protected by land charge; they fall outside the land charges system and are governed by the doctrine of notice. C: Wrong because the doctrine of notice has not been abolished; it still applies to equitable interests over unregistered land that cannot be protected by land charge (including trust interests and pre-1926 restrictive covenants and easements). A: Wrong because constructive and imputed notice are sufficient; Thomas does not need to have personal actual knowledge. D: Wrong because overreaching only occurs when the purchase money is paid to at least two trustees; if the money is paid to only one trustee, overreaching does not take place.

questions.tag: Joint tenancy vs tenancy in common
questions.scenario: Adam, Beth, and Cara buy a property together. The transfer deed expressly states that they hold the beneficial interest 'in equal shares'. All four unities are present.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes how the equitable title is held?

options.A: As a tenancy in common, because the words 'in equal shares' indicate that the parties intend to hold separate shares.
options.B: As a joint tenancy, because all four unities are present.
options.C: As a joint tenancy, because the right of survivorship always applies where the four unities are present.
options.D: As a tenancy in common, but only because there are three co-owners rather than two.
options.E: As a joint tenancy, because the shares are described as 'equal', which is consistent with joint tenancy.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Although all four unities are present (which would normally indicate a joint tenancy), the use of the words 'in equal shares' is an express declaration that the parties intend to hold as tenants in common. Following Pink v Lawrence, an express declaration that the owners hold as tenants in common will prevail even if the four unities are present. Words such as 'in equal shares' or 'equally' indicate that the parties intend to have identifiable shares, which is characteristic of a tenancy in common. B: Wrong because the presence of all four unities does not automatically create a joint tenancy where there is an express declaration or wording indicating a tenancy in common. C: Wrong because survivorship does not always apply; it only applies to joint tenancies, and the express wording here creates a tenancy in common. D: Wrong because the number of co-owners is irrelevant to whether the equitable title is held as a joint tenancy or tenancy in common; it depends on the unities and any express declaration. E: Wrong because 'equal shares' specifically denotes separate quantifiable interests, which is the hallmark of a tenancy in common, not a joint tenancy.

questions.scenario: Edward and Frances are equitable joint tenants of their home. Following a relationship breakdown, Frances instructs her solicitors to send a letter to Edward at their shared home stating: 'Our client hereby gives notice that she wishes to sever the joint tenancy of the above property immediately.' Edward is away on a business trip when the letter is delivered. He does not read it for two weeks.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes whether severance has occurred?

options.A: Severance has not occurred because Edward did not receive the letter personally.
options.B: Severance has not occurred because Edward did not read the letter before the two-week period expired.
options.C: Severance has not occurred because the notice must be signed by Frances personally, not sent by solicitors.
options.D: Severance has occurred because the notice demonstrates an unequivocal and irrevocable intention to sever immediately, and it was delivered to Edward's last known place of abode.
options.E: Severance has occurred, but only from the date Edward actually read the letter.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Under s 36(2) LPA 1925, an equitable joint tenancy can be severed by notice in writing. The notice must demonstrate an unequivocal and irrevocable intention to sever immediately (Re Draper's Conveyance). Under s 196(3) LPA 1925, a notice is deemed served if it is left at the last known place of abode of the joint tenant. Following Kinch v Bullard, the notice takes effect on delivery — it does not need to have been read. E: Wrong because personal receipt is not required; delivery to the last known place of abode is sufficient under s 196(3) LPA 1925. A: Wrong because severance occurs on delivery, not on reading; Kinch v Bullard confirms that even destruction of the letter after delivery does not undo the severance. C: Wrong because there are no particular formalities for the notice — it does not need to be signed, and it can be sent by solicitors on the joint tenant's behalf. B: Wrong because severance takes effect at the moment of delivery, not the moment of reading; this was confirmed in Kinch v Bullard.

questions.scenario: Gemma and Hugo are equitable joint tenants of a flat. Gemma writes a will leaving 'my share of the flat' to her niece, Isla. Gemma then dies.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes who is entitled to Gemma's interest in the flat?

options.A: Isla is entitled to Gemma's interest because Gemma's will expressly leaves it to her.
options.B: Hugo is entitled to the whole flat by survivorship, because the right of survivorship operates automatically on death and overrides any provision in a will.
options.C: Isla and Hugo share the flat equally, with Isla taking Gemma's share under the will and Hugo retaining his own.
options.D: Gemma's interest passes under the intestacy rules because a will cannot dispose of jointly held property.
options.E: Hugo is entitled to the flat, but only if he can prove that Gemma did not intend to sever the joint tenancy by writing the will.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Where there is a joint tenancy, the right of survivorship (ius accrescendi) applies automatically on the death of a joint tenant. The deceased's notional interest accrues to the surviving joint tenant(s). A will has no effect until after death, but by that point survivorship has already operated. Following Re Caines deceased, any provision in a will purporting to leave a joint tenant's interest to someone else will have no effect. A: Wrong because a joint tenant's interest cannot be left by will; survivorship takes priority and operates automatically on death. C: Wrong because there is no 'share' for Isla to inherit; under a joint tenancy, the co-owners are a single entity and the deceased's interest accrues to the survivor automatically. D: Wrong because the interest does not pass under intestacy rules either; survivorship operates before both wills and intestacy rules. E: Wrong because the making of a will does not sever a joint tenancy (Re Caines deceased); severance must take place during the joint tenant's lifetime, and a will only takes effect after death.

questions.scenario: Jack and Kate are equitable joint tenants of their home. Without Kate's knowledge, Jack takes out a mortgage over his equitable interest in the property to raise money for a business venture.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the effect of Jack's mortgage on the joint tenancy?

options.A: The joint tenancy is unaffected because a mortgage taken by one joint tenant has no effect on the equitable title.
options.B: The mortgage is void because a joint tenant cannot deal with their equitable interest without the consent of the other joint tenant.
options.C: The joint tenancy is severed, but only if Kate consents to the mortgage.
options.D: The joint tenancy is severed and Jack loses his entire interest in the property to the lender.
options.E: The joint tenancy is severed because Jack's mortgage over his equitable interest is an act of partial alienation, which operates as a unilateral act of severance.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Under Williams v Hensman, severance can occur through a unilateral act by one joint tenant operating on their own share. A mortgage over an equitable interest is an act of partial alienation — the severing owner temporarily disposes of the interest. This severs the joint tenancy without requiring the knowledge or consent of the other joint tenant. Following First National Security v Hegarty, a mortgage of a joint tenant's equitable interest will sever the joint tenancy. A: Wrong because a mortgage of an equitable interest does affect the equitable title by severing the joint tenancy; it is only where all legal joint tenants act together to mortgage the legal estate that the equitable estate remains unaffected. C: Wrong because severance by unilateral act does not require the other joint tenant's consent or knowledge; the act itself severs. D: Wrong because the mortgage does not cause Jack to lose his entire interest; it creates a charge over his severed share as security, which he can recover by repaying the loan. B: Wrong because a joint tenant can deal with their equitable interest unilaterally; the act of doing so severs the joint tenancy.

questions.scenario: Nina and Oliver bought a house together as a family home. They are co-owners, and the property is held on trust. Following a separation, Nina moves out. She applies to the court under TOLATA s 14 for an order for sale. Oliver opposes the sale because their 10-year-old daughter, Polly, lives with him in the property.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the approach the court will take?

options.A: The court will automatically order a sale because Nina, as a beneficiary, has an absolute right to realise her interest.
options.B: The court will refuse a sale because the property is a family home and family homes can never be sold under TOLATA.
options.C: The court will apply all relevant factors in s 15 TOLATA, including the purpose for which the property is held and the welfare of any minor occupying or expected to occupy the property as their home.
options.D: The court will order Oliver to buy out Nina's share because that is the only order available under s 14.
options.E: The court has no jurisdiction to hear the application because Nina has moved out and is no longer in occupation.

questions.explanation: C is correct. Under s 14 TOLATA, any person with an interest in land subject to a trust can apply for a court order. Under s 15, the court must consider all relevant factors including: the purposes for which the property is held (s 15(1)(b)) — here a family home, which may weigh against sale if the purpose continues; and the welfare of any minor who occupies or might reasonably be expected to occupy the property as their home (s 15(1)(c)) — Polly is 10 years old and lives in the property. The court cannot pick and choose which factors to apply. B: Wrong because there is no automatic right to a sale; the court exercises discretion having considered all the s 15 factors. D: Wrong because the court can order a sale of a family home; it is simply one factor to consider that the purpose of the trust may still be continuing. A: Wrong because the court cannot order one beneficiary to buy out another under s 14; the available orders include sale, postponement of sale, orders as to occupation, and orders as to the nature or extent of a beneficiary's interest. E: Wrong because s 14(1) allows any person with an interest in the property to apply; Nina does not need to be in occupation to make an application.

questions.scenario: Victoria, the freehold owner of a warehouse, allows William to occupy the premises under an agreement stating that William may remain 'for as long as the building works on the adjacent site are ongoing'. William pays rent monthly.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the status of this arrangement?

options.A: The arrangement is a valid fixed-term lease because it has a defined start point and will end when the building works are completed.
options.B: The arrangement is void and William has no right to remain on the premises.
options.C: The arrangement is a valid lease because the duration can be estimated based on the expected timeline for the building works.
options.D: The arrangement is automatically a yearly periodic tenancy because William pays rent.
options.E: The arrangement fails as a lease for lack of certainty of term because it is not known when the building works will end, but an implied periodic tenancy may arise from the monthly payment and acceptance of rent.

questions.explanation: E is correct. Under Street v Mountford, a lease requires certainty of term. Following Lace v Chantler, a term tied to an uncertain future event ('for as long as the building works are ongoing') fails for lack of certainty because it is not known at the outset when the arrangement will end. However, following Prudential Assurance v London Residuary Body, where a person is in possession and paying rent on a periodic basis, an implied periodic tenancy may arise. The monthly payment and acceptance of rent would create an implied monthly periodic tenancy. A: Wrong because the term is not fixed; it depends on an uncertain future event (the completion of the building works), which fails the certainty of term requirement. C: Wrong because an estimated timeline does not satisfy certainty of term; the maximum duration must be known or ascertainable from the outset. B: Wrong because the type of implied periodic tenancy depends on the period by reference to which the rent is calculated, not simply that rent is paid; here the rent is paid monthly, so a monthly periodic tenancy would arise, not a yearly one. D: Wrong because although the arrangement fails as a fixed-term lease, William is not left without any rights; an implied periodic tenancy arises from his possession and the payment and acceptance of rent.

questions.scenario: Phoebe occupies a one-bedroom flat owned by Gregory. The written agreement is labelled a 'licence'. Gregory retains a key to the flat but has never used it except once when Phoebe asked him to water her plants while she was on holiday. The agreement requires Phoebe to pay a monthly sum described as a 'licence fee'. No services are provided by Gregory.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the true nature of this arrangement?

options.A: The arrangement is likely a lease because, applying Street v Mountford, the substance of the arrangement shows exclusive possession and a certain term, regardless of the label used.
options.B: The arrangement is a licence because Gregory retains a key to the flat.
options.C: The arrangement is a licence because it is labelled as such and the payments are described as 'licence fees'.
options.D: The arrangement is a licence because Phoebe allowed Gregory to enter to water her plants, which shows she does not have exclusive possession.
options.E: The arrangement is a lease only if Phoebe can show she pays market rent.

questions.explanation: A is correct. Under Street v Mountford, the court looks at the substance of the arrangement, not the label. Parties cannot alter the effect of an arrangement by calling it a licence. Here, Phoebe has exclusive possession of a one-bedroom flat (Gregory has never used the key except by Phoebe's invitation), there is a certain term (implied from the monthly payments), and the label and description of payments are irrelevant. C: Wrong because Street v Mountford established that the label given to an arrangement is irrelevant; the court examines the substance. B: Wrong because retention of a key does not automatically defeat exclusive possession; following Aslan v Murphy, the purpose for which the key is retained is the important factor. If the key is only used in emergencies or by arrangement, exclusive possession may still exist. D: Wrong because allowing entry by arrangement (to water plants at Phoebe's request) does not defeat exclusive possession; Phoebe controlled access to the flat. E: Wrong because under s 205(1)(xxvii) LPA 1925, rent is not essential for a lease, as confirmed in Ashburn Anstalt v Arnold; the payment of any sum is relevant evidence but market rent is not a requirement.

questions.scenario: Anya and her partner Ben occupy a two-bedroom flat. The landlord, Clive, insists they each sign separate agreements labelled 'licences'. Each agreement includes a clause stating: 'The licensor reserves the right to introduce additional persons to share the premises with the licensee.' Clive has never exercised this clause. The flat would not realistically accommodate additional occupants.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the effect of the sharing clause?

options.A: The sharing clause is effective and means Anya and Ben are licensees because the landlord has reserved a right to share.
options.B: The sharing clause defeats exclusive possession because it gives Clive an unrestricted right to introduce others.
options.C: The sharing clause is valid because separate agreements always create separate licences.
options.D: The sharing clause is likely a sham and will be disregarded by the court, meaning Anya and Ben probably have a joint lease.
options.E: The sharing clause is effective because Clive is the landlord and has an absolute right to determine the nature of the agreement.

questions.explanation: D is correct. Following AG Securities v Vaughan and Antoniades v Villiers, the court looks at all circumstances to determine whether a sharing clause is genuine or a sham designed to defeat exclusive possession. Relevant factors include the size and nature of the accommodation (a two-bedroom flat would not realistically accommodate additional occupants), the relationship between the occupiers (they are a couple, making it inappropriate to introduce strangers), the width of the drafting (widely drafted clauses are more likely shams), and whether the clause has ever been exercised (it has not).
