These factors strongly suggest the clause is a sham. The separate agreements are identical and interdependent, and following Antoniades v Villiers, separate documents do not defeat unity of title if they are interdependent. A: Wrong because a sharing clause is not automatically effective; the court examines whether it is genuine or a sham using the Antoniades factors. B: Wrong because the clause will be disregarded if it is a sham; the court looks at the reality of the situation, not just the terms of the agreement. C: Wrong because separate agreements do not necessarily create separate licences; if they are identical and interdependent, the court treats them as one arrangement (Antoniades v Villiers). E: Wrong because the landlord cannot determine the legal nature of the arrangement merely by the terms they impose; Street v Mountford requires the court to look at the substance.

questions.scenario: Zara, a freehold owner, orally agrees to let a room in her property to Theo for two years at market rent. Theo moves in immediately and starts paying rent monthly. No written document is created.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the status of Theo's arrangement?

options.A: Theo has no lease because all leases must be created by deed under s 52 LPA 1925.
options.B: Theo has a legal lease under the short lease exception in s 54(2) LPA 1925 because the lease is for three years or less, takes effect in possession, and is at market rent with no premium.
options.C: Theo has an equitable lease because the oral agreement satisfies s 2 LP(MP)A 1989.
options.D: Theo has a legal lease, but only for one month at a time as an implied periodic tenancy.
options.E: Theo has no lease because oral agreements for land are always void.

questions.explanation: B is correct. Under s 54(2) LPA 1925, a lease with a term of three years or less does not need to be created by deed provided three conditions are met: (1) the lease takes effect in possession — Theo moved in immediately; (2) the lease is granted at best rent (market rent) — this is satisfied; (3) there is no fine or premium — no upfront payment. All three conditions are met, so Theo has a valid legal lease despite the absence of any written document. This is known as a parol lease. A: Wrong because s 54(2) is an exception to the general rule in s 52 LPA 1925 that legal interests must be created by deed; short leases meeting the conditions do not require a deed. C: Wrong because s 2 LP(MP)A 1989 requires writing, and the agreement here is oral; in any event, s 54(2) provides a direct route to a legal lease without the need for writing. D: Wrong because the arrangement is not merely an implied periodic tenancy; there is a clear oral agreement for a fixed term of two years which satisfies s 54(2). E: Wrong because s 54(2) LPA 1925 specifically permits certain short leases to be created orally.

questions.scenario: Maxwell is employed as a caretaker at Riverside School. As part of his employment, he is required to live in a flat within the school building so he can respond to emergencies and maintain security outside school hours. He pays a reduced monthly sum from his salary.
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the nature of Maxwell's occupation?

options.A: Maxwell has a lease because he pays a monthly sum and has exclusive possession of the flat.
options.B: Maxwell has a licence because the arrangement is between an employer and employee.
options.C: Maxwell has a service occupancy, not a lease, because he is required to live in the premises for the better performance of his duties as an employee.
options.D: Maxwell has a lease because the payment of any rent automatically creates a tenancy.
options.E: Maxwell has a service occupancy, but only if the flat is owned by the school and not by a third party.

questions.explanation: C is correct. A service occupancy arises where there is an employer/employee relationship and the occupier is required to live in the premises for the better performance of their duties as an employee. Following Norris v Checksfield, a requirement to occupy the premises to perform duties (here, responding to emergencies and maintaining security) creates a service occupancy, not a lease, even if rent is paid. This is distinguished from Royal Philanthropic v County, where a teacher living near a school had a lease because the occupation did not help him better perform his duties. A: Wrong because even if Maxwell pays a monthly sum and has exclusive possession, the service occupancy exception defeats the lease where occupation is required for the better performance of duties. B: Wrong because not every arrangement between an employer and employee is automatically a licence; a service occupancy is a specific category that requires the occupation to be for the better performance of duties. D: Wrong because the payment of rent does not automatically create a tenancy; the service occupancy exception applies regardless of whether rent is paid. E: Wrong because the ownership of the flat by a third party is not the relevant factor; what matters is whether the employee is required to live in the premises for the better performance of their duties.
